item.dc:title: Gold‐Catalyzed Photoannulation: Access to Cyclic Scaffolds

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Influence of 4,6‐O‐Benzylidine Towards α‐Galactosylation, Overcoming Neighbouring Group Participation Effect: An Unanticipated Observation With Viable Solution
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
During N‐iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate‐mediated β‐galactosylation, an unexpected formation of α‐glycoside was observed with a series of D‐Glucosamine‐derived acceptors. The unexpected stereo outcome was attributed to the 4,6‐O‐benzylidine in the donor molecule, and the expected β‐glycoside was formed upon removal of this benzylidine protecting group.
The construction of β‐glycosidic linkages with the assistance of neighbouring group participation (NGP) during N‐iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate‐mediated glycosylation reactions is considered one of the most reliable reactions. In this study, we demonstrated that the 4,6‐O‐benzylidine protection in the galactosyl thioglycoside donor can overshadow the NGP effect, furnishing the unexpected α‐glycoside with a specific set of acceptors and donors. We synthesized an orthogonally protected galactosyl thioglycoside donor to overcome the unexpected stereoselectivity. The outcome revealed that the presence of 4,6‐O‐benzylidine protection on the donor moiety can alter the stereoselectivity during glycosylation in certain cases.
item.content:encoded:
During N-iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate-mediated β-galactosylation, an unexpected formation of α-glycoside was observed with a series of D-Glucosamine-derived acceptors. The unexpected stereo outcome was attributed to the 4,6-O-benzylidine in the donor molecule, and the expected β-glycoside was formed upon removal of this benzylidine protecting group.


The construction of β-glycosidic linkages with the assistance of neighbouring group participation (NGP) during N-iodosuccinimide and trimethylsilyl trifluoromethanesulfonate-mediated glycosylation reactions is considered one of the most reliable reactions. In this study, we demonstrated that the 4,6-O-benzylidine protection in the galactosyl thioglycoside donor can overshadow the NGP effect, furnishing the unexpected α-glycoside with a specific set of acceptors and donors. We synthesized an orthogonally protected galactosyl thioglycoside donor to overcome the unexpected stereoselectivity. The outcome revealed that the presence of 4,6-O-benzylidine protection on the donor moiety can alter the stereoselectivity during glycosylation in certain cases.
item.dc:creator: Swapnendu Pramanik, Mrinmoy Manash Bharali, Abhishek Santra
item.dc:title: Influence of 4,6‐O‐Benzylidine Towards α‐Galactosylation, Overcoming Neighbouring Group Participation Effect: An Unanticipated Observation With Viable Solution

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic Synthesis of Imidazoline and Oxazolidine Derivatives Using Organic Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we report a synthetic strategy that relies on a self‐developed organic photocatalyst for the efficient construction of imidazolines and oxazolidines. Using readily available 4‐methyl‐N‐(2 phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox‐active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox‐initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate‐type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.
Herein, we report a synthetic strategy that relies on a self‐developed organic photocatalyst for the efficient construction of imidazolines and oxazolidines. Using readily available 4‐methyl‐N‐(2‐phenylallyl)benzenesulfonamide alkenes and redox‐active esters (RAEs) or aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborates as substrates, the reaction proceeds in acetonitrile/acetone via photoredox‐initiated radical addition followed by ionic cyclization, which involves the formation of a key nitrilium/imidate‐type ionic intermediate. This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

This transformation features mild reaction conditions and facile operation, not only expanding the application scope of the developed photocatalyst, but also providing a practical technical platform for the photocatalytic synthesis of structurally diverse bioactive molecules containing imidazoline and oxazolidine motifs.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Difluorocarbene as C Synthon and CF Synthon in Cycloadditions and Annulations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes the progress since 2018 in the application of difluorocarbene as either a C1 synthon or a CF synthon in cyclization reactions. The content is organized according to the distinct functions of difluorocarbene, provides a detailed overview of its role as C1 synthon for the synthesis of over twenty distinct heterocyclic scaffolds, and as CF synthon for the construction of monofluorinated heterocyclic frameworks, such as 2‐fluoroindoles, 3‐fluorinated oxindoles and indolizines.
This review summarizes the progress in the application of difluorocarbene as a multifunctional fluorinated reagent in cyclization reactions via the cleavage of C–F bonds, with a focus on research since 2018. The contents are organized according to the functions exhibited by difluorocarbene, and reaction mechanisms are provided to illustrate the multifaceted roles of this important building block in various transformations by selective C–F bond cleavage, including its use as a C1 synthon or CF synthon.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes the progress since 2018 in the application of difluorocarbene as either a C1 synthon or a CF synthon in cyclization reactions. The content is organized according to the distinct functions of difluorocarbene, provides a detailed overview of its role as C1 synthon for the synthesis of over twenty distinct heterocyclic scaffolds, and as CF synthon for the construction of monofluorinated heterocyclic frameworks, such as 2-fluoroindoles, 3-fluorinated oxindoles and indolizines.


This review summarizes the progress in the application of difluorocarbene as a multifunctional fluorinated reagent in cyclization reactions via the cleavage of C–F bonds, with a focus on research since 2018. The contents are organized according to the functions exhibited by difluorocarbene, and reaction mechanisms are provided to illustrate the multifaceted roles of this important building block in various transformations by selective C–F bond cleavage, including its use as a C1 synthon or CF synthon.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: One‐Pot Synthesis of Unexplored Furo[3,2‐c]coumarin‐Pyrazine Hybrids: A Catalyst‐Free Isocyanide‐Mediated Multicomponent Annulation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We describe an annulative multicomponent reaction based on isocyanides that synthesizes fuzed furo[3,2‐c]coumarin‐pyrazine heterocycles. This one‐pot method effectively creates novel heterocycles, including nitrogen and oxygen, by using 4‐hydroxycoumarins, pyrazine‐2‐aldehydes, and isocyanides in the absence of metals and additives. Multiple CC and Cheteroatom bonds are formed in a single operation with good to excellent yields as the transformation proceeds via a cascade process under mild conditions. The technique shows promise for post‐synthetic modification, and gram‐scale application.
We herein report an annulative isocyanide‐based multicomponent approach for the synthesis of fuzed furo[3,2‐c] coumarin‐pyrazine heterocycles. The reaction involves a one‐pot strategy employing pyrazine‐2‐aldehydes, 4‐hydroxycoumarins, and isocyanides, affording annulated oxygen‐ and nitrogen‐containing frameworks without the use of metal catalysts or additives. The transformation allows the construction of multiple heterocyclic bonds in a single operation and proceeds efficiently under mild thermal conditions. The strategy features a wide array of substrate scope, with the desired fuzed heterocycles produced in good to excellent yields. The protocol enables efficient gram‐scale synthesis with possible post‐synthetic modification of the heterocyclic core. Considering the known biological relevance of both furocoumarin and pyrazine motifs, this methodology provides a useful entry to previously unexplored fuzed heterocyclic scaffolds.
item.content:encoded:
We describe an annulative multicomponent reaction based on isocyanides that synthesizes fuzed furo[3,2-c]coumarin-pyrazine heterocycles. This one-pot method effectively creates novel heterocycles, including nitrogen and oxygen, by using 4-hydroxycoumarins, pyrazine-2-aldehydes, and isocyanides in the absence of metals and additives. Multiple CC and Cheteroatom bonds are formed in a single operation with good to excellent yields as the transformation proceeds via a cascade process under mild conditions. The technique shows promise for post-synthetic modification, and gram-scale application.


We herein report an annulative isocyanide-based multicomponent approach for the synthesis of fuzed furo[3,2-c] coumarin-pyrazine heterocycles. The reaction involves a one-pot strategy employing pyrazine-2-aldehydes, 4-hydroxycoumarins, and isocyanides, affording annulated oxygen- and nitrogen-containing frameworks without the use of metal catalysts or additives. The transformation allows the construction of multiple heterocyclic bonds in a single operation and proceeds efficiently under mild thermal conditions. The strategy features a wide array of substrate scope, with the desired fuzed heterocycles produced in good to excellent yields. The protocol enables efficient gram-scale synthesis with possible post-synthetic modification of the heterocyclic core. Considering the known biological relevance of both furocoumarin and pyrazine motifs, this methodology provides a useful entry to previously unexplored fuzed heterocyclic scaffolds.

item.dc:title: One‐Pot Synthesis of Unexplored Furo[3,2‐c]coumarin‐Pyrazine Hybrids: A Catalyst‐Free Isocyanide‐Mediated Multicomponent Annulation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mild Oxidative Synthesis of N‐Formamides With Bio‐Based Pyruvaldehyde in Green Solvents
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The application of pyruvaldehyde as a formyl equivalent for the N‐formylation of amines in ethanol under mild reaction conditions has been developed. This method features broad substrate scope, good functional group tolerance, and generate products in good yields.
This work represents a catalyst‐free green protocol for the N‐formylation of amines using pyruvaldehyde as a formyl source in ethanol under mild reaction conditions. This highly selective, catalyst‐free, and base‐free protocol enables the efficient synthesis of diverse formamides in moderate to excellent yields. It also offers a broad substrate scope, accommodating both structural and electronic variations of amines and amine‐containing heterocycles, thereby facilitating diversity generation. The avoidance of hazardous waste and the feasibility of gram‐scale synthesis represent prominent additional advantages.
item.content:encoded:
The application of pyruvaldehyde as a formyl equivalent for the N-formylation of amines in ethanol under mild reaction conditions has been developed. This method features broad substrate scope, good functional group tolerance, and generate products in good yields.

This highly selective, catalyst-free, and base-free protocol enables the efficient synthesis of diverse formamides in moderate to excellent yields. It also offers a broad substrate scope, accommodating both structural and electronic variations of amines and amine-containing heterocycles, thereby facilitating diversity generation. The avoidance of hazardous waste and the feasibility of gram-scale synthesis represent prominent additional advantages.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: 1,1‐Disubstituted Vinylbromides: Versatile Building Blocks for the Synthesis of Nitrogen‐Containing Heterocycles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
1,1‐Disubstituted vinylbromides are key intermediates for constructing nitrogen‐containing heterocycles. This review provides an overview of the synthetic applications of 1,1‐disubstituted vinylbromides and summarizes recent developments in reaction methodologies, mechanistic insights, and the structural diversity of N‐heterocyclic compounds accessible through these versatile intermediates.
1,1‐Disubstituted vinyl bromides have emerged as powerful and versatile building blocks for the synthesis of nitrogen‐containing heterocycles. Their dual functionality—encompassing both a reactive bromide and a polarized CC bond—enables diverse transformations including cycloadditions, metal‐catalyzed couplings, lithiation, and radical or cascade processes. This review summarizes recent advances up to 2025 in the preparation of aziridines, pyrroles, indoles, indolizines, pyrrolidines, pyrazoles, triazoles, isoxazoles, isoxazolidines, and piperidines derived from these substrates. Particular attention is given to regio‐ and stereoselective strategies, the construction of fluorinated and functionalized heterocycles, and applications in medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
1,1-Disubstituted vinylbromides are key intermediates for constructing nitrogen-containing heterocycles. This review provides an overview of the synthetic applications of 1,1-disubstituted vinylbromides and summarizes recent developments in reaction methodologies, mechanistic insights, and the structural diversity of N-heterocyclic compounds accessible through these versatile intermediates.


1,1-Disubstituted vinyl bromides have emerged as powerful and versatile building blocks for the synthesis of nitrogen-containing heterocycles. Their dual functionality—encompassing both a reactive bromide and a polarized CC bond—enables diverse transformations including cycloadditions, metal-catalyzed couplings, lithiation, and radical or cascade processes. This review summarizes recent advances up to 2025 in the preparation of aziridines, pyrroles, indoles, indolizines, pyrrolidines, pyrazoles, triazoles, isoxazoles, isoxazolidines, and piperidines derived from these substrates. Particular attention is given to regio- and stereoselective strategies, the construction of fluorinated and functionalized heterocycles, and applications in medicinal chemistry.

item.dc:title: 1,1‐Disubstituted Vinylbromides: Versatile Building Blocks for the Synthesis of Nitrogen‐Containing Heterocycles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Design, Synthesis, and Photophysical Studies of Functionalized Polyaromatic Isonitriles as Visible Light Photoredox Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A new series of structurally rich polyaromatic isonitriles is presented as photoredox catalysts. Their photophysical properties are investigated by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence measurements, combined with experimental and theoretical studies to evaluate both ground‐ and excited‐state redox properties.
The chemistry of imidoyl radicals derived from single‐electron transfer to an isonitrile has been gaining increasing attention, thanks to their multifaceted reactivity. The latter involves the ability of aromatic isonitriles to harvest visible light, reach an electronically excited state, and trigger the formation of open‐shell species from suitable radical precursors. We herein report the synthesis of structurally rich polyaromatic isonitriles, the characterization of their photophysical properties via UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence measurements, along with combined experimental and theoretical studies to evaluate both ground and excited‐state redox properties. Finally, synthetic validation as photoredox catalysts enabled to highlight the best‐performing isonitrile‐based scaffolds, holding promise for further developments in the field.
item.content:encoded:
A new series of structurally rich polyaromatic isonitriles is presented as photoredox catalysts. Their photophysical properties are investigated by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence measurements, combined with experimental and theoretical studies to evaluate both ground- and excited-state redox properties.


The chemistry of imidoyl radicals derived from single-electron transfer to an isonitrile has been gaining increasing attention, thanks to their multifaceted reactivity. The latter involves the ability of aromatic isonitriles to harvest visible light, reach an electronically excited state, and trigger the formation of open-shell species from suitable radical precursors. We herein report the synthesis of structurally rich polyaromatic isonitriles, the characterization of their photophysical properties via UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence measurements, along with combined experimental and theoretical studies to evaluate both ground and excited-state redox properties. Finally, synthetic validation as photoredox catalysts enabled to highlight the best-performing isonitrile-based scaffolds, holding promise for further developments in the field.
item.dc:creator: Cristina Martini, Camilla Russo, Jussara Amato, Carmine Volpe, Francesca Brunelli, Laurence Grimaud, Maxime R. Vitale, Greta Donati, Andrea Basso, Mariateresa Giustiniano
item.dc:title: Design, Synthesis, and Photophysical Studies of Functionalized Polyaromatic Isonitriles as Visible Light Photoredox Catalysts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Unlocking the Functionalization and Reactivity of Ethylene Bridges in [2.2]Paracyclophanes: Strategies and Challenges
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Bridging the gap. Beyond deck functionalization, the ethylene bridges of [2.2]paracyclophanes offer a largely unexploited reactivity landscape. This review showcases strategies to functionalize these bridges and leverage them to build novel cyclophane architectures.
[2.2]Paracyclophanes (pCps) are rigid scaffolds consisting of two aromatic rings held in a cofacial arrangement by ethylene bridges. This distinctive geometry enforces through‐space π−π interactions, confers unusual reactivity, ensures configurational stability, and imparts planar chirality onto the systems. While functionalization of the aromatic rings is well‐established, selective modification of the ethylene bridges remains a major synthetic challenge. This review surveys current strategies for the preparation of bridge‐functionalized pCps, including construction of the cyclophane core from substituted precursors, direct derivatization of the parent scaffold, and functionalization of strained or deck‐substituted derivatives. Particular emphasis is placed on exploiting bridge reactivity to access novel molecular architectures, especially via contractive annulation approaches. Representative examples demonstrate how radical, photochemical, and stepwise transformations enable the installation of diverse substituents, the formation of π‐extended motifs at bridge positions, and the assembly of unprecedented strained polycyclic systems. Despite notable progress, challenges in yield, selectivity, and scalability remain, and enantiopure bridge‐functionalized [2.2]pCps are still largely unexplored. Continued methodological development is therefore expected to expand the utility of pCps as versatile building blocks for advanced materials, organometallic ligands, and catalytic systems, positioning the bridge as a strategic platform for accessing new structural and functional possibilities in contemporary cyclophane chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
Bridging the gap. Beyond deck functionalization, the ethylene bridges of [2.2]paracyclophanes offer a largely unexploited reactivity landscape. This review showcases strategies to functionalize these bridges and leverage them to build novel cyclophane architectures.


[2.2]Paracyclophanes (pCps) are rigid scaffolds consisting of two aromatic rings held in a cofacial arrangement by ethylene bridges. This distinctive geometry enforces through-space π−π interactions, confers unusual reactivity, ensures configurational stability, and imparts planar chirality onto the systems. While functionalization of the aromatic rings is well-established, selective modification of the ethylene bridges remains a major synthetic challenge. This review surveys current strategies for the preparation of bridge-functionalized pCps, including construction of the cyclophane core from substituted precursors, direct derivatization of the parent scaffold, and functionalization of strained or deck-substituted derivatives. Particular emphasis is placed on exploiting bridge reactivity to access novel molecular architectures, especially via contractive annulation approaches. Representative examples demonstrate how radical, photochemical, and stepwise transformations enable the installation of diverse substituents, the formation of π-extended motifs at bridge positions, and the assembly of unprecedented strained polycyclic systems. Despite notable progress, challenges in yield, selectivity, and scalability remain, and enantiopure bridge-functionalized [2.2]pCps are still largely unexplored. Continued methodological development is therefore expected to expand the utility of pCps as versatile building blocks for advanced materials, organometallic ligands, and catalytic systems, positioning the bridge as a strategic platform for accessing new structural and functional possibilities in contemporary cyclophane chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Rongyu Sun, Tamara Ye, Laurent Micouin, Erica Benedetti
item.dc:title: Unlocking the Functionalization and Reactivity of Ethylene Bridges in [2.2]Paracyclophanes: Strategies and Challenges

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Porcine Liver Esterase‐Catalyzed Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of a Lactone‐Bridged Biaryl: Impact of Organic Cosolvents on Enantioselectivity
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A stereolabile lactone‐bridged biaryl enables dynamic kinetic resolution to provide a conformationally stable ring‐opened biaryl. A significant positive effect of water‐immiscible organic cosolvents on the enantioselectivity in a reaction catalyzed by porcine liver esterase has been discovered for the first time.
Driven by the growing interest in the synthesis of enantiomerically pure biaryl compounds, we report herein a commercial porcine liver esterase (PLE)‐catalyzed atroposelective ring‐opening reaction of a racemic lactone‐bridged biaryl, providing a biaryl carboxylic acid. A distinctive feature of this method is that the substrate bearing low rotational barrier (ΔG‡rot = 21.9 kcal/mol) undergoes rapid racemization at 30°C, whereas the resulting ring‐opened product is conformationally stable (ΔG‡rot = 30.5 kcal/mol). Consequently, the intrinsic rotational properties of both the substrate and the product enable a dynamic kinetic resolution without the need for an external racemization catalyst. Remarkably, the use of a mixture of a water‐immiscible organic cosolvent, such as cyclopentyl methyl ether, and a buffer solution as reaction medium significantly increased the enantioselectivity of the PLE‐catalyzed reaction to give the biaryl product (>90% yield) with 65% ee. Furthermore, it was concluded that among the several isoenzymes present in the commercial crude PLE, the lactone hydrolysis is primarily catalyzed by its isoenzymes PLE‐5 and PLE‐6.
item.content:encoded:
A stereolabile lactone-bridged biaryl enables dynamic kinetic resolution to provide a conformationally stable ring-opened biaryl. A significant positive effect of water-immiscible organic cosolvents on the enantioselectivity in a reaction catalyzed by porcine liver esterase has been discovered for the first time.


Driven by the growing interest in the synthesis of enantiomerically pure biaryl compounds, we report herein a commercial porcine liver esterase (PLE)-catalyzed atroposelective ring-opening reaction of a racemic lactone-bridged biaryl, providing a biaryl carboxylic acid. A distinctive feature of this method is that the substrate bearing low rotational barrier (ΔG ‡ rot = 21.9 kcal/mol) undergoes rapid racemization at 30°C, whereas the resulting ring-opened product is conformationally stable (ΔG ‡ rot = 30.5 kcal/mol). Consequently, the intrinsic rotational properties of both the substrate and the product enable a dynamic kinetic resolution without the need for an external racemization catalyst. Remarkably, the use of a mixture of a water-immiscible organic cosolvent, such as cyclopentyl methyl ether, and a buffer solution as reaction medium significantly increased the enantioselectivity of the PLE-catalyzed reaction to give the biaryl product (>90% yield) with 65% ee. Furthermore, it was concluded that among the several isoenzymes present in the commercial crude PLE, the lactone hydrolysis is primarily catalyzed by its isoenzymes PLE-5 and PLE-6.
item.dc:creator: Neha Dhiman, Anke Hummel, Kyohei Kanomata, Yoshinari Sawama, Tohru Taniguchi, Harald Gröger, Shuji Akai
item.dc:title: Porcine Liver Esterase‐Catalyzed Dynamic Kinetic Resolution of a Lactone‐Bridged Biaryl: Impact of Organic Cosolvents on Enantioselectivity

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Annulation of Chromene‐2‐Carboxamides With Maleimides via CH Activation: Synthesis of Functionalized Spiro Bis‐γ‐Lactams
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a robust asymmetric tandem [4 + 1] annulation to synthesize functionalized spiro bis‐γ‐lactams. Ring‐opening of the chromone moiety in the products yields multisubstituted spiro bis‐γ‐lactams, enabling efficient construction of a diverse library of such biologically potential derivatives and facilitating relevant synthetic research.
We report an efficient tandem [4 + 1] annulation method for synthesizing functionalized spiro bis‐γ‐lactams from chromone‐2‐carboxamides and maleimides. These spiro compounds integrate chromone, pyrrolidone, and unique spirocyclic skeletons in one molecular framework, endowing them with diverse structural characteristics. Moreover, the chromone ring‐opening reaction of these products provides access to another class of spiro bis‐γ‐lactams bearing multiple substituents. This approach enables the efficient construction of a diverse library of potentially bioactive spiro bis‐γ‐lactam derivatives and serves as a valuable platform for related synthetic studies.
item.content:encoded:
We report a robust asymmetric tandem [4 + 1] annulation to synthesize functionalized spiro bis-γ-lactams. Ring-opening of the chromone moiety in the products yields multisubstituted spiro bis-γ-lactams, enabling efficient construction of a diverse library of such biologically potential derivatives and facilitating relevant synthetic research.


We report an efficient tandem [4 + 1] annulation method for synthesizing functionalized spiro bis-γ-lactams from chromone-2-carboxamides and maleimides. These spiro compounds integrate chromone, pyrrolidone, and unique spirocyclic skeletons in one molecular framework, endowing them with diverse structural characteristics. Moreover, the chromone ring-opening reaction of these products provides access to another class of spiro bis-γ-lactams bearing multiple substituents. This approach enables the efficient construction of a diverse library of potentially bioactive spiro bis-γ-lactam derivatives and serves as a valuable platform for related synthetic studies.
item.dc:creator: Si‐Yi Chen, Qing‐Sheng Zhao, Wei Peng, Cong‐Hai Zhang, Sheng‐Jiao Yan
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Annulation of Chromene‐2‐Carboxamides With Maleimides via CH Activation: Synthesis of Functionalized Spiro Bis‐γ‐Lactams

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Intramolecular NN Bond Formations to Access N‐Heterocycles Under Transition Metal‐Free Conditions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Transition metal–free intramolecular N–N bond formation is a promising route to access N‐heterocycles. This review highlights advances from 2015 to 2025 across electrochemical, photochemical, I2‐promoted, hypervalent iodine‐mediated, and N‐halosuccinimide platforms, discussing reaction conditions, scope, limitations, mechanistic studies, and late‐stage applications. Emerging design principles and unresolved challenges are highlighted to spur new NN‐forming strategies.
Discovery of versatile approaches for the construction of high‐valued N‐rich heterocycles has been a very important research topic in organic chemistry for several years. Owing to their widespread applications in natural products, pharmaceuticals, dye industry, and materials, a multitude of efficient methods to access N‐heterocyclic scaffolds have been uncovered. Of them, transition metal‐free intramolecular NN bond‐forming strategies are distinctive category of transformations and highly preferred because of their wider scope and versatility. In this review, we summarized the existing literature to access N‐heterocycles involving transition metal‐free intramolecular NN bond‐forming approaches from 2015 to 2025. Methodological advancements reported under (1) electrochemical, (2) photochemical, (3) I2‐promoted, (4) hypervalent iodine‐mediated, and (5) N‐halo succinimide‐based conditions are explored. Reaction conditions, representative examples, limitations, post‐synthetic applications, and mechanism are discussed wherever applicable. We expect that the synthetic approaches discussed herein will serve as a comprehensive reference tool and enable further expansion in this field.
item.content:encoded:
Transition metal–free intramolecular N–N bond formation is a promising route to access N-heterocycles. This review highlights advances from 2015 to 2025 across electrochemical, photochemical, I2-promoted, hypervalent iodine-mediated, and N-halosuccinimide platforms, discussing reaction conditions, scope, limitations, mechanistic studies, and late-stage applications. Emerging design principles and unresolved challenges are highlighted to spur new NN-forming strategies.


Discovery of versatile approaches for the construction of high-valued N-rich heterocycles has been a very important research topic in organic chemistry for several years. Owing to their widespread applications in natural products, pharmaceuticals, dye industry, and materials, a multitude of efficient methods to access N-heterocyclic scaffolds have been uncovered. Of them, transition metal-free intramolecular NN bond-forming strategies are distinctive category of transformations and highly preferred because of their wider scope and versatility. In this review, we summarized the existing literature to access N-heterocycles involving transition metal-free intramolecular NN bond-forming approaches from 2015 to 2025. Methodological advancements reported under (1) electrochemical, (2) photochemical, (3) I2-promoted, (4) hypervalent iodine-mediated, and (5) N-halo succinimide-based conditions are explored. Reaction conditions, representative examples, limitations, post-synthetic applications, and mechanism are discussed wherever applicable. We expect that the synthetic approaches discussed herein will serve as a comprehensive reference tool and enable further expansion in this field.

item.dc:title: Intramolecular NN Bond Formations to Access N‐Heterocycles Under Transition Metal‐Free Conditions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Perimidine Directed C−H Activation/Annulation for the Synthesis of Hexacyclic Cationic Azaperylenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Perimidine has been used as directing group to induce Rh(III)‐catalyzed C−H activation of 2‐aryl‐1H‐perimidines and [4 + 2] annulation with alkynes, leading to hexacyclic cationic azaperylenes A in good to high yields with broad scope. They could also be easily afforded by three‐component triple C−H annulation from all commercial raw materials (naphthalene‐1,8‐diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes). These cationic PAHs show tunable (green to red) luminescence with general > 540 nm emissions.
Annulative π‐extension for the synthesis of cationic azapyrenes has been widely researched, while access to larger π‐systems such as azaperylenes is rare. Herein, perimidine is reported as a fresh directing group to trigger Rh(III)‐catalyzed dual C−H activation/annulation of 2‐aryl perimidines and alkynes, constructing hexacyclic perimidinium salts (benzo‐azaperylenes) A. Salts A could also be synthesized via three‐component triple C−H annulation from commercial naphthalene‐1,8‐diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes. A hexacyclic BF2 complex B1 is conveniently prepared from HO‐substituted perimidine. Three rhodacycles have been detected by ESI‐MS to support the proposed mechanism. The photophysical properties, such as absorptions, emissions, and cyclic voltammetry of these novel cations, are characterized. Salts A show tunable luminescence (516–602 nm) affected by the group from perimidine more than alkyne, and BF2 complex B1 shows emission peaking at 602 nm. The counteranions also have influence on the luminescence, with an order of Cl > BF4 > OTf > SbF6.
item.content:encoded:
Perimidine has been used as directing group to induce Rh(III)-catalyzed C−H activation of 2-aryl-1H-perimidines and [4 + 2] annulation with alkynes, leading to hexacyclic cationic azaperylenes A in good to high yields with broad scope. They could also be easily afforded by three-component triple C−H annulation from all commercial raw materials (naphthalene-1,8-diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes). These cationic PAHs show tunable (green to red) luminescence with general > 540 nm emissions.


Annulative π-extension for the synthesis of cationic azapyrenes has been widely researched, while access to larger π-systems such as azaperylenes is rare. Herein, perimidine is reported as a fresh directing group to trigger Rh(III)-catalyzed dual C−H activation/annulation of 2-aryl perimidines and alkynes, constructing hexacyclic perimidinium salts (benzo-azaperylenes) A. Salts A could also be synthesized via three-component triple C−H annulation from commercial naphthalene-1,8-diamine, aldehydes, and alkynes. A hexacyclic BF2 complex B1 is conveniently prepared from HO-substituted perimidine. Three rhodacycles have been detected by ESI-MS to support the proposed mechanism. The photophysical properties, such as absorptions, emissions, and cyclic voltammetry of these novel cations, are characterized. Salts A show tunable luminescence (516–602 nm) affected by the group from perimidine more than alkyne, and BF2 complex B1 shows emission peaking at 602 nm. The counteranions also have influence on the luminescence, with an order of Cl > BF4 > OTf > SbF6.
item.dc:creator: Hui Li, Haidan Gao, Mingming Yu, Dan Yang, Shiqing Li, Jinhua Wang
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed Perimidine Directed C−H Activation/Annulation for the Synthesis of Hexacyclic Cationic Azaperylenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed Alkoxy Radical‐Mediated Ring‐Expansion/C(sp3)–N Coupling of Hemiketal Peroxides with N‐Nucleophiles: A Route to Functionalized Macrolactones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed alkoxy radical‐mediated ring‐expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of fused hemiketal peroxides with various N‐nucleophiles is developed. This method provides efficient access to functionalized medium‐sized and macrolactones bearing an amino fragment at a defined position. The reaction proceeds under mild, redox‐neutral conditions with broad substrate scope, and the leaving group of the peroxide is crucial for the radical initiation step.
A copper‐catalyzed alkoxy radical‐mediated ring‐expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of hemiketal peroxides with N‐nucleophiles has been developed. This protocol allows the construction of functionalized medium‐sized and macrolactones under mild redox‐neutral conditions, exhibiting broad substrate scope and good functional group tolerance. The ring‐expansion amination proceeds efficiently with diverse nitrogen nucleophiles, including amides, sulfamides, indoles, indazoles, and pyrazoles, providing a concise and step‐economical strategy for the synthesis of structurally diverse amino‐substituted lactones. Control studies reveal that the nature of the leaving group in hemiketal peroxides is crucial for the success of this reaction.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-catalyzed alkoxy radical-mediated ring-expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of fused hemiketal peroxides with various N-nucleophiles is developed. This method provides efficient access to functionalized medium-sized and macrolactones bearing an amino fragment at a defined position. The reaction proceeds under mild, redox-neutral conditions with broad substrate scope, and the leaving group of the peroxide is crucial for the radical initiation step.


A copper-catalyzed alkoxy radical-mediated ring-expansion/C(sp3)–N coupling of hemiketal peroxides with N-nucleophiles has been developed. This protocol allows the construction of functionalized medium-sized and macrolactones under mild redox-neutral conditions, exhibiting broad substrate scope and good functional group tolerance. The ring-expansion amination proceeds efficiently with diverse nitrogen nucleophiles, including amides, sulfamides, indoles, indazoles, and pyrazoles, providing a concise and step-economical strategy for the synthesis of structurally diverse amino-substituted lactones. Control studies reveal that the nature of the leaving group in hemiketal peroxides is crucial for the success of this reaction.
item.dc:creator: Wenke Li, Hong‐Fei Liu, Xin‐Hua Duan, Li‐Na Guo
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed Alkoxy Radical‐Mediated Ring‐Expansion/C(sp3)–N Coupling of Hemiketal Peroxides with N‐Nucleophiles: A Route to Functionalized Macrolactones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photocatalytic and Palladium‐Catalyzed Synthesis of Thioglycoside‐1,2,4‐Triazole Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A dual photochemical and Pd‐catalyzed approach allows the selective di‐thioglyco‐conjugation of 1,2,4‐triazoles with thiosugars. This method exploits complementary reactivity pathways, providing access to new thioglycosides scaffolds under mild conditions.
This study presents an efficient approach for the di‐functionalization of 1,2,4‐triazoles with thiosugars, combining photochemical processes and metal‐catalyzed coupling reactions under mild conditions. Exploiting the dual reactivity of the selected 1,2,4‐triazoles, this strategy enables selective and versatile transformations, affording a new family of 1,2,4‐triazole derivatives conjugated with thiosugars with potential for further exploration in biologically relevant studies.
item.content:encoded:
A dual photochemical and Pd-catalyzed approach allows the selective di-thioglyco-conjugation of 1,2,4-triazoles with thiosugars. This method exploits complementary reactivity pathways, providing access to new thioglycosides scaffolds under mild conditions.


This study presents an efficient approach for the di-functionalization of 1,2,4-triazoles with thiosugars, combining photochemical processes and metal-catalyzed coupling reactions under mild conditions. Exploiting the dual reactivity of the selected 1,2,4-triazoles, this strategy enables selective and versatile transformations, affording a new family of 1,2,4-triazole derivatives conjugated with thiosugars with potential for further exploration in biologically relevant studies.

item.dc:title: Photocatalytic and Palladium‐Catalyzed Synthesis of Thioglycoside‐1,2,4‐Triazole Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Diastereoselective Synthesis of Chiral Nitrogen Heterocycles via Reactions of N‐(tert‐Butylsulfinyl) Polyfluoroalkyl Aldimines With Heteroatom‐Substituted Dienes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A catalyst‐free, stereocontrolled addition of Rawal's diene to enantiopure fluorinated aldimines affords diastereomerically pure enaminones. The latter are easily converted into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones and pyrazoles, bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino group. A diastereoselective hetero‐Diels–Alder reaction of Ellman`s fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords N‐sulfinyl‐protected 6‐trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridones.
A stereocontrolled hetero‐Diels–Alder reaction of enantiomeric Ellman's fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords diastereomerically pure N‐sulfinyl‐protected 6‐trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridone. In addition, a highly diastereoselective, catalyst‐free aza‐Mukaiyama addition of Rawal's diene to the CN bond of enantiomeric fluorine‐containing aldimines, RFCHNS(O)tert‐Bu (RF = CF3, CHF2, CF2Me, CF2Ph), has been developed, providing diastereomerically pure enaminones. These chemically and configurationally stable enaminones are cleanly converted under acidic conditions into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino fragment at the 6‐position. Furthermore, cyclocondensation of the enaminones with hydrazines affords the corresponding pyrazoles incorporating a stereochemically pure fluoroalkylated amino group.
item.content:encoded:
A catalyst-free, stereocontrolled addition of Rawal's diene to enantiopure fluorinated aldimines affords diastereomerically pure enaminones. The latter are easily converted into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones and pyrazoles, bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino group. A diastereoselective hetero-Diels–Alder reaction of Ellman`s fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords N-sulfinyl-protected 6-trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridones.


A stereocontrolled hetero-Diels–Alder reaction of enantiomeric Ellman's fluoral aldimine with Danishefsky's diene affords diastereomerically pure N-sulfinyl-protected 6-trifluoromethyltetrahydropyridone. In addition, a highly diastereoselective, catalyst-free aza-Mukaiyama addition of Rawal's diene to the CN bond of enantiomeric fluorine-containing aldimines, RFCHNS(O)tert-Bu (RF = CF3, CHF2, CF2Me, CF2Ph), has been developed, providing diastereomerically pure enaminones. These chemically and configurationally stable enaminones are cleanly converted under acidic conditions into enantiomeric tetrahydropyridones bearing a chiral fluoroalkylated amino fragment at the 6-position. Furthermore, cyclocondensation of the enaminones with hydrazines affords the corresponding pyrazoles incorporating a stereochemically pure fluoroalkylated amino group.
item.dc:creator: Yuliya V. Rassukana, Roman M. Zakharko, Lyudmyla V. Bezghubenko, Alona S. Cherednichenko, Svitlana V. Shishkina, Petro P. Onys'ko
item.dc:title: Highly Diastereoselective Synthesis of Chiral Nitrogen Heterocycles via Reactions of N‐(tert‐Butylsulfinyl) Polyfluoroalkyl Aldimines With Heteroatom‐Substituted Dienes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Review of Nitration of Anilines: Toward More Sustainable Approaches
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Nitroanilines are important intermediates that can be synthesized through aromatic nitration of anilines. To address the drawbacks of the established mixed acids nitration, various strategies have been developed offering unique selectivity and functional tolerance. A particular emphasis is given to the development of more sustainable pathways using enabling technologies while unveiling their area of improvement to supplant the mixed acids paradigm.
Nitroanilines are versatile building blocks for the synthesis of miscellaneous compounds such as dyes, pharmaceuticals, perfumes and many other targets. To answer the chemical limitations of the well‐developed “mixed acids” conditions (such as harsh conditions, poor regioselectivity, and poor functional group tolerance), several alternatives nitration conditions have been developed specifically for aniline derivatives. In this review, we offer an overview of the miscellaneous existing methodologies to provide nitroanilines through aromatic nitration of pre‐functionalized anilines. The development of more sustainable approaches using enabling technologies is also presented and their sustainability is discussed through a comparative study of E‐factors.
item.content:encoded:
Nitroanilines are important intermediates that can be synthesized through aromatic nitration of anilines. To address the drawbacks of the established mixed acids nitration, various strategies have been developed offering unique selectivity and functional tolerance. A particular emphasis is given to the development of more sustainable pathways using enabling technologies while unveiling their area of improvement to supplant the mixed acids paradigm.


Nitroanilines are versatile building blocks for the synthesis of miscellaneous compounds such as dyes, pharmaceuticals, perfumes and many other targets. To answer the chemical limitations of the well-developed “mixed acids” conditions (such as harsh conditions, poor regioselectivity, and poor functional group tolerance), several alternatives nitration conditions have been developed specifically for aniline derivatives. In this review, we offer an overview of the miscellaneous existing methodologies to provide nitroanilines through aromatic nitration of pre-functionalized anilines. The development of more sustainable approaches using enabling technologies is also presented and their sustainability is discussed through a comparative study of E-factors.

item.dc:title: A Review of Nitration of Anilines: Toward More Sustainable Approaches

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photochemical Decatungstate‐Catalyzed Hydroacylation of Maleimides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A photocatalytic protocol to access substituted succinimides via tetra‐n‐butylammonium decatungstate photocatalysis is reported. This direct radical‐driven hydroacylation of a variety of N‐substituted maleimides shows tolerance to various aldehydes (or alkanes), affording differentially substituted succinimides in high yields.
Succinimides constitute a useful and coveted structural scaffold in organic synthesis, due to their wide range of applications. Herein, we present an efficient approach for the synthesis of substituted succinimides by means of photocatalysis. Namely, tetra n‐butylammonium decatungstate, a well‐known photocatalyst, was employed under light irradiation, in order to synthesize a variety of succinimide ketones via a direct hydroacylation reaction of N‐substituted maleimides. A diverse range of aldehydes and N‐substituted maleimides show great compatibility with this protocol, providing functionalized succinimides in satisfactory yields. The protocol is applicable to flow conditions, as well as in a gram scale reaction.
item.content:encoded:
A photocatalytic protocol to access substituted succinimides via tetra-n-butylammonium decatungstate photocatalysis is reported. This direct radical-driven hydroacylation of a variety of N-substituted maleimides shows tolerance to various aldehydes (or alkanes), affording differentially substituted succinimides in high yields.


Succinimides constitute a useful and coveted structural scaffold in organic synthesis, due to their wide range of applications. Herein, we present an efficient approach for the synthesis of substituted succinimides by means of photocatalysis. Namely, tetra n-butylammonium decatungstate, a well-known photocatalyst, was employed under light irradiation, in order to synthesize a variety of succinimide ketones via a direct hydroacylation reaction of N-substituted maleimides. A diverse range of aldehydes and N-substituted maleimides show great compatibility with this protocol, providing functionalized succinimides in satisfactory yields. The protocol is applicable to flow conditions, as well as in a gram scale reaction.
item.dc:creator: Manos V. G. Lantzanakis, Naya A. Stini, Christoforos G. Kokotos
item.dc:title: Photochemical Decatungstate‐Catalyzed Hydroacylation of Maleimides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed, Strain‐Promoted Synthesis of Imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from Cyclic Diaryliodonium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A highly efficient Cu‐catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodoniums. This protocol enables the late‐stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules.
A highly efficient copper‐catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridinium derivatives from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodonium salts. Notably, this protocol is suitable for the late‐stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules. Mechanistic studies indicate that the reaction proceeds via a tandem strain‐promoted ring‐opening/intramolecular cyclization sequence.
item.content:encoded:
A highly efficient Cu-catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2-f]phenanthridiniums from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodoniums. This protocol enables the late-stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules.


A highly efficient copper-catalyzed method has been developed for constructing valuable imidazo[1,2-f]phenanthridinium derivatives from readily available imidazoles and torsionally strained cyclic diaryliodonium salts. Notably, this protocol is suitable for the late-stage functionalization of imidazole motifs in complex drug molecules. Mechanistic studies indicate that the reaction proceeds via a tandem strain-promoted ring-opening/intramolecular cyclization sequence.
item.dc:creator: Kai Zhu, Yi Zheng, Lei Yang, Yanqi Wu, Fengzhi Zhang
item.dc:title: Copper‐Catalyzed, Strain‐Promoted Synthesis of Imidazo[1,2‐f]phenanthridiniums from Cyclic Diaryliodonium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Chain Elongation Reaction of Tetrahydrofuran for the Synthesis of Cinnamate Esters
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Visible‐light‐induced three‐component reactions of cinnamic acid, diazoesters, and tetrahydrofuran/acetonitrile have been developed for the synthesis of cinnamic acid esters. The synthetic methods, which are performed under mild conditions, feature broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance.
A visible‐light‐induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester is developed. In the presence of THF, this reaction provided chain elongated cinnamic acid esters via formation and ring opening of oxonium ylide with cinnamic acid. The same light‐induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester in MeCN provided simple esters of cinnamic acid.
item.content:encoded:
Visible-light-induced three-component reactions of cinnamic acid, diazoesters, and tetrahydrofuran/acetonitrile have been developed for the synthesis of cinnamic acid esters. The synthetic methods, which are performed under mild conditions, feature broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance.


A visible-light-induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester is developed. In the presence of THF, this reaction provided chain elongated cinnamic acid esters via formation and ring opening of oxonium ylide with cinnamic acid. The same light-induced reaction of cinnamic acid and diazoester in MeCN provided simple esters of cinnamic acid.
item.dc:creator: Deep J. Kalita, Rajtonoy Bharali, Srinivas Ambala, Sanjib Gogoi
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Chain Elongation Reaction of Tetrahydrofuran for the Synthesis of Cinnamate Esters

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances of Photo/Electro‐Enzymatic Catalytic Systems in Organic Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in integrating photochemistry, electrochemistry, and enzyme catalysis for organic synthesis, focusing on three multicatalytic systems: photo‐enzymatic, electro‐enzymatic, and photo‐electro‐enzymatic catalysis. Their mechanisms, advantages, and challenges are discussed, along with future directions in catalyst design, system optimization, mechanistic studies, and industrial applications for green synthesis.
In recent years, the integration of photochemistry, electrochemistry, and enzyme catalysis has brought revolutionary advances to the field of organic synthesis. This multicatalytic synergistic strategy not only significantly enhances reaction efficiency and selectivity but also demonstrates great potential in green synthesis, chiral construction, carbon–carbon/carbon–heteroatom bond formation, and the synthesis of complex molecules. This review systematically summarizes the applications of three multicatalytic systems—photo‐enzymatic catalysis, electro‐enzymatic catalysis, and photo‐electro‐enzymatic catalysis—in organic synthesis, and discusses the mechanistic features, advantages, and challenges of each system. Finally, the future development directions in the field are outlined, including catalyst design, system optimization, mechanistic studies, and industrial applications, to further advance this green synthetic strategy.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in integrating photochemistry, electrochemistry, and enzyme catalysis for organic synthesis, focusing on three multicatalytic systems: photo-enzymatic, electro-enzymatic, and photo-electro-enzymatic catalysis. Their mechanisms, advantages, and challenges are discussed, along with future directions in catalyst design, system optimization, mechanistic studies, and industrial applications for green synthesis.


In recent years, the integration of photochemistry, electrochemistry, and enzyme catalysis has brought revolutionary advances to the field of organic synthesis. This multicatalytic synergistic strategy not only significantly enhances reaction efficiency and selectivity but also demonstrates great potential in green synthesis, chiral construction, carbon–carbon/carbon–heteroatom bond formation, and the synthesis of complex molecules. This review systematically summarizes the applications of three multicatalytic systems—photo-enzymatic catalysis, electro-enzymatic catalysis, and photo-electro-enzymatic catalysis—in organic synthesis, and discusses the mechanistic features, advantages, and challenges of each system. Finally, the future development directions in the field are outlined, including catalyst design, system optimization, mechanistic studies, and industrial applications, to further advance this green synthetic strategy.
item.dc:creator: Shuangshuang Wu, Xintao He, Yujie Zhan, Wufu Zhu, Jianye Zhang, Hong Zhang
item.dc:title: Recent Advances of Photo/Electro‐Enzymatic Catalytic Systems in Organic Synthesis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Julolidine‐Functionalized Amphiphilic Aza‐BODIPY Dyes: Synthesis, Near‐Infraed‐II Emission, and pH‐Response
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Four 1,7‐julolidine‐substituted amphiphilic aza‐BODIPY dyes 1a‐d bearing hydrophilic side chains at the 3,5‐phenyls were synthesized. Modulating the number, position, and structure of electron‐donating linkages (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) on the 3,5‐phenyl rings of these dyes enables tunable optical properties with NIR II fluorescence with up to 1400 nm (dye 1b in DMSO, emission maximum = 1109 nm).
A series of amphiphilic aza‐BODIPY dyes (1a‐d) bearing two julolidine units at 1,7‐positions of the azadipyrromethene core and hydrophilic oligo(ethylene glycol) chains at the 3,5‐phenyls were synthesized and characterized. All dyes feature broad, panchromatic absorption bands from 350 nm up to 1000–1100 nm in CH2Cl2 and exhibit near‐infrared (NIR‐II) emission bands up to 1400 nm. Solvent‐dependent absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic studies reveal pronounced positive solvatochromism of these dyes and further Lippert–Mataga analyses reveal their efficient intramolecular charge transfer character. The optical properties of these dyes are strongly influenced by the substitution patterns as well as the linker structures (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) for the hydrophilic moieties at the 3,5‐phenyls, which is further elucidated by density functional theory calculations. Notably, dye 1b exhibits the most red‐shifted emission (1109 nm in DMSO) with a high fluorescence quantum yield (2.0%). In addition, all dyes display reversible spectroscopic pH‐response. These results underscore the promising application potential of these new aza‐BODIPY dyes.
item.content:encoded:
Four 1,7-julolidine-substituted amphiphilic aza-BODIPY dyes 1a-d bearing hydrophilic side chains at the 3,5-phenyls were synthesized. Modulating the number, position, and structure of electron-donating linkages (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) on the 3,5-phenyl rings of these dyes enables tunable optical properties with NIR II fluorescence with up to 1400 nm (dye 1b in DMSO, emission maximum = 1109 nm).


A series of amphiphilic aza-BODIPY dyes (1a-d) bearing two julolidine units at 1,7-positions of the azadipyrromethene core and hydrophilic oligo(ethylene glycol) chains at the 3,5-phenyls were synthesized and characterized. All dyes feature broad, panchromatic absorption bands from 350 nm up to 1000–1100 nm in CH2Cl2 and exhibit near-infrared (NIR-II) emission bands up to 1400 nm. Solvent-dependent absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic studies reveal pronounced positive solvatochromism of these dyes and further Lippert–Mataga analyses reveal their efficient intramolecular charge transfer character. The optical properties of these dyes are strongly influenced by the substitution patterns as well as the linker structures (–OCH2– or –N(CH2)2) for the hydrophilic moieties at the 3,5-phenyls, which is further elucidated by density functional theory calculations. Notably, dye 1b exhibits the most red-shifted emission (1109 nm in DMSO) with a high fluorescence quantum yield (2.0%). In addition, all dyes display reversible spectroscopic pH-response. These results underscore the promising application potential of these new aza-BODIPY dyes.
item.dc:creator: Mengyu Hao, Hongfei Pan, Xuefeng Kuang, Hanjun Huang, Xiang‐Kui Ren, Zhijian Chen
item.dc:title: Julolidine‐Functionalized Amphiphilic Aza‐BODIPY Dyes: Synthesis, Near‐Infraed‐II Emission, and pH‐Response

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Selective Synthesis of Spirocyclobutanes via [2 + 2] Cycloaddition of α,β‐Unsaturated Acenaphthenone and Acenaphthylene
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A visible‐light‐driven [2 + 2] photocycloaddition enables the efficient construction of spirocyclobutane scaffolds from α,β‐unsaturated acenaphthylenones and acenaphthylene under mild conditions. High diastereoselectivity (up to dr > 99:1) is achieved. Product selectivity is governed by substrate structure, and the products are readily isolated by simple filtration without chromatography.
The cyclobutane framework serves as an important class of rigid scaffolds with broad applications in medicinal chemistry and advanced materials. In particular, spirocyclobutanes have emerged as a key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules owing to their distinctive three‐dimensional geometry and enhanced metabolic stability. Herein, we report an efficient and practical synthetic strategy that constructs the spirocyclobutane core in a single step via a visible‐light‐promoted, copper sulfate‐catalyzed intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition between α,β‐unsaturated acenaphthenones and acenaphthylene. This operationally simple protocol requires only straightforward filtration to afford products in high purity. Meanwhile, the good functional group tolerance and broad substrate scope were well demonstrated by delivering the diverse target spirocyclobutanes in moderate to excellent yields (50%–92%) with high stereoselectivity. This novel method thus offers a practical, economical, and sustainable alternative for the construction of spirocyclobutane derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
A visible-light-driven [2 + 2] photocycloaddition enables the efficient construction of spirocyclobutane scaffolds from α,β-unsaturated acenaphthylenones and acenaphthylene under mild conditions. High diastereoselectivity (up to dr > 99:1) is achieved. Product selectivity is governed by substrate structure, and the products are readily isolated by simple filtration without chromatography.


The cyclobutane framework serves as an important class of rigid scaffolds with broad applications in medicinal chemistry and advanced materials. In particular, spirocyclobutanes have emerged as a key pharmacophore in numerous bioactive molecules owing to their distinctive three-dimensional geometry and enhanced metabolic stability. Herein, we report an efficient and practical synthetic strategy that constructs the spirocyclobutane core in a single step via a visible-light-promoted, copper sulfate-catalyzed intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition between α,β-unsaturated acenaphthenones and acenaphthylene. This operationally simple protocol requires only straightforward filtration to afford products in high purity. Meanwhile, the good functional group tolerance and broad substrate scope were well demonstrated by delivering the diverse target spirocyclobutanes in moderate to excellent yields (50%–92%) with high stereoselectivity. This novel method thus offers a practical, economical, and sustainable alternative for the construction of spirocyclobutane derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Yuqing Sun, Zhaojuan Lan, Bing Sun, Fang‐Lin Zhang
item.dc:title: Highly Selective Synthesis of Spirocyclobutanes via [2 + 2] Cycloaddition of α,β‐Unsaturated Acenaphthenone and Acenaphthylene

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Versatile Synthesis of Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol and Related Compounds by Protecting‐Group‐Free Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An operationally simple method for synthesizing Δ9‐tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9‐THC) and its analogs via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling provides rapid access to diverse Δ9‐THC analogs, with potential applications for affording authentic standards of suspect compounds in forensic contexts with limited facilities.
Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9‐THC) was synthesized via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling of a triflate precursor, derived from an inexpensive terpenoid and phloroglucinol, with a boronic acid derivative. This method also provides easy access to Δ9‐THC derivatives bearing a variety of n‐alkyl, aryl and alkenyl side chains and is applicable to other cannabinoids, including cannabidiol and Δ8‐THC derivatives, without requiring strictly anhydrous conditions, special equipment, or protection of the phenolic hydroxyl group. It is expected to be useful in preparing authentic standards of suspected illicit drugs in forensic laboratories where expertise and facilities for organic synthesis may be limited.
item.content:encoded:

Δ9-Tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9-THC) was synthesized via Suzuki–Miyaura coupling of a triflate precursor, derived from an inexpensive terpenoid and phloroglucinol, with a boronic acid derivative. This method also provides easy access to Δ9-THC derivatives bearing a variety of n-alkyl, aryl and alkenyl side chains and is applicable to other cannabinoids, including cannabidiol and Δ8-THC derivatives, without requiring strictly anhydrous conditions, special equipment, or protection of the phenolic hydroxyl group. It is expected to be useful in preparing authentic standards of suspected illicit drugs in forensic laboratories where expertise and facilities for organic synthesis may be limited.

item.dc:title: Versatile Synthesis of Δ9‐Tetrahydrocannabinol and Related Compounds by Protecting‐Group‐Free Suzuki–Miyaura Coupling

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Divergent Synthesis of Polycyclic Derivatives via Gold Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Gold(I)‐catalyzed cycloisomerization enables the formation of complex 3D structures from simple enyne precursors. This study presents a divergent strategy to synthesize functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone. Reaction conditions control the pathway (6‐endo‐dig or 5‐exo‐dig), leading to different molecular architectures, including bicyclo and tricyclo derivatives. Mechanistic insights highlight the role of intermediates, solvent, and substituents, offering an efficient route to rigid polycyclic compounds of interest in medicinal chemistry.
Gold(I)‐catalyzed cycloisomerization reactions provide powerful access to structurally complex and 3D molecular frameworks from simple enyne precursors. Herein, we report a divergent gold‐catalyzed strategy for the synthesis of densely functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic polycyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone platform. Depending on the reaction conditions, solvent, and catalyst, the transformation proceeded through either a 6‐endo‐dig or a 5‐exo‐dig cyclization pathway, leading to distinct molecular architectures. Under selected conditions, bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane derivatives were obtained with high selectivity, while alternative conditions promote skeletal rearrangements delivering bridged tricyclic frameworks, including tricyclo[3.2.1.02,7]octanes and tricyclo[3.3.1.02,8]nonanes. According to literature mechanistic investigations, the involvement and evolution of key vinyl–gold and gold carbene intermediates may govern regioselectivity and product distribution. The role of solvent effects and substituent electronics in controlling pathway divergence was highlighted. This gold‐catalyzed divergent approach enabled rapid molecular complexity generation and provided efficient access to rigid, 3D polycyclic scaffolds of potential interest for medicinal chemistry and drug discovery applications.
item.content:encoded:

This study presents a divergent strategy to synthesize functionalized bicyclic and tricyclic scaffolds from a common alkynyl ketone. Reaction conditions control the pathway (6-endo-dig or 5-exo-dig), leading to different molecular architectures, including bicyclo and tricyclo derivatives. Mechanistic insights highlight the role of intermediates, solvent, and substituents, offering an efficient route to rigid polycyclic compounds of interest in medicinal chemistry.

Under selected conditions, bicyclo[3.3.1]nonane derivatives were obtained with high selectivity, while alternative conditions promote skeletal rearrangements delivering bridged tricyclic frameworks, including tricyclo[3.2.1.02,7]octanes and tricyclo[3.3.1.02,8]nonanes. The role of solvent effects and substituent electronics in controlling pathway divergence was highlighted. item.dc:title: Divergent Synthesis of Polycyclic Derivatives via Gold Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Zwitterionic C–N Bond Cleavage by the Accumulation of Charge: Green Synthesis of Amino Thiazolyl Benzimidazolium Bromide and Benzimidazol‐Thiazole under Microwave Irradiations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The reaction was carried out for the C–N bond cleavage by the accumulation of charge. The zwitterionic intermediate stabilized by cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and accumulation of negative charge at highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases the HOMO‐LUMO band gap and resulted to C–N bond cleavage. L‐proline was used as organocatalyst under microwave irradiations.
The Zwitterionic curious methodology for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage to synthesize the anticancer substituted amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and its thiazole. The cyclic hydrogen bonding (multiple hydrogen bonding) approach with accumulation of charge have been applied for the regioselective C–N bond cleavage by organocatalysis. It is metal free one‐pot reaction fascinated by microwave irradiations. The zwitterionic L‐proline have been proposed as catalyst and acceptor partner in donor–acceptor cyclic hydrogen bonding system. The work is green and sustainable without separation, purification and solvent extraction. The respective accumulation of positive charge and negative charge at lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) decreases energy gap in cyclic hydrogen bonding. The accumulation of positive charge on two carbons executed the C–N bond cleavage effectively by the migration of benzimidazole moiety. The density functional theory is used to study the intermediates using HOMO‐LUMO frontier orbital concept. This elite cyclic hydrogen bonding between bromide salt and neutral thiazole moiety abetted the solidification of product in solution. The synthesized products are anticancer agents. All products created new library of amino thiazolyl benzimidazolium bromide and their thiazole heterocycles.

L-proline was used as organocatalyst under microwave irradiations.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: 1,2‐Diazetidines − Structure, Synthesis, and Functionalization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
1,2‐Diazetidines are saturated four‐membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite recent advances in their synthesis and promising potential in medicinal chemistry, the chemistry of these cyclic hydrazines remains underexplored. This review highlights their three‐dimensional structure and explores various strategies for their preparation and functionalization.
1,2‐Diazetidines are saturated four‐membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite their promising potential as rigid molecular scaffolds for drug discovery, their synthesis and reactivity remain largely unexplored. This review summarizes their three‐dimensional structural features, which have been characterized using various spectroscopic and crystallographic methods. Current synthetic approaches of 1,2‐diazetidines fall into three main categories: (i) [2 + 2] cycloadditions between azo compounds and electron‐rich olefins, (ii) photochemical ring contraction of dihydropyridazines, and (iii) intramolecular ring closure of hydrazine derivatives, the latter enabling stereocontrol at the four‐membered ring. Finally, the reactivity and functionalization of these cyclic hydrazines are discussed, including derivatization at both nitrogen atoms.
item.content:encoded:

Despite recent advances in their synthesis and promising potential in medicinal chemistry, the chemistry of these cyclic hydrazines remains underexplored. This review highlights their three-dimensional structure and explores various strategies for their preparation and functionalization.


1,2-Diazetidines are saturated four-membered heterocycles featuring two adjacent nitrogen atoms. Despite their promising potential as rigid molecular scaffolds for drug discovery, their synthesis and reactivity remain largely unexplored. This review summarizes their three-dimensional structural features, which have been characterized using various spectroscopic and crystallographic methods. Current synthetic approaches of 1,2-diazetidines fall into three main categories: (i) [2 + 2] cycloadditions between azo compounds and electron-rich olefins, (ii) photochemical ring contraction of dihydropyridazines, and (iii) intramolecular ring closure of hydrazine derivatives, the latter enabling stereocontrol at the four-membered ring. Finally, the reactivity and functionalization of these cyclic hydrazines are discussed, including derivatization at both nitrogen atoms.
item.dc:creator: Stefan Roesner
item.dc:title: 1,2‐Diazetidines − Structure, Synthesis, and Functionalization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Maleimides Revisited: Reactivity, Selectivity, and Emerging Synthetic Strategies
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Maleimides are presented as electronically tunable α,β‐unsaturated platforms whose reactivity extends far beyond classical Michael chemistry. Recent advances in catalytic C–H activation, conjugate addition, and cycloaddition strategies enable precise control of regioselectivity, stereoselectivity, and π‐system retention. Emerging hybrid approaches expand their role as adaptable building blocks for complex molecule construction and translational applications.
Maleimides are α,β‐unsaturated cyclic imides that serve as electronically tunable α,β‐unsaturated scaffolds enabling controlled bond construction. Recent advances have redefined maleimides from simple Michael acceptors into programmable electrophilic platforms that enable controlled regio‐, stereo‐, and chemoselective bond formation. Over the past 5 years, this versatile scaffold has seen renewed attention in forming CC, CN, and CS bonds through transition‐metal‐catalyzed alkylation, alkenylation, organocatalytic and metal‐catalyzed conjugate additions, and diverse cycloaddition reactions. This review summarizes recent synthetic approaches to maleimide construction and functionalization, with particular focus on nucleophilic additions involving carbon and heteroatom pronucleophiles, enantioselective metal‐catalyzed transformations including C–H activation, and [4 + 2], [3 + 2], and [2 + 2] cycloaddition strategies leading to structurally complex frameworks. Emphasis is placed on methodologies that address long‐standing challenges in maleimide chemistry, including control of selectivity, retention of unsaturation, and expansion of post‐functionalization through emerging and hybrid catalytic strategies.
item.content:encoded:

Recent advances in catalytic C–H activation, conjugate addition, and cycloaddition strategies enable precise control of regioselectivity, stereoselectivity, and π-system retention. Emerging hybrid approaches expand their role as adaptable building blocks for complex molecule construction and translational applications.

Over the past 5 years, this versatile scaffold has seen renewed attention in forming CC, CN, and CS bonds through transition-metal-catalyzed alkylation, alkenylation, organocatalytic and metal-catalyzed conjugate additions, and diverse cycloaddition reactions. This review summarizes recent synthetic approaches to maleimide construction and functionalization, with particular focus on nucleophilic additions involving carbon and heteroatom pronucleophiles, enantioselective metal-catalyzed transformations including C–H activation, and [4 + 2], [3 + 2], and [2 + 2] cycloaddition strategies leading to structurally complex frameworks. Emphasis is placed on methodologies that address long-standing challenges in maleimide chemistry, including control of selectivity, retention of unsaturation, and expansion of post-functionalization through emerging and hybrid catalytic strategies.
