item.dc:title: Maleimides Revisited: Reactivity, Selectivity, and Emerging Synthetic Strategies

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed Ring‐Opening Halogenation of Spiro‐Dihydroquinazolinones Using Perhalogenated Methanes as Halogen Sources
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cascade reaction involving spiro C—C σ–bond cleavage and C–halogen bond formation is reported for the efficient construction of bromo‐/chloroalkyl‐substituted quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones from spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones and perhalogenated methanes under organophotoredox catalysis.
Visible‐light‐catalyzed aromatization‐driven deconstructive bromination and chlorination of spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones are described, with perhalogenated methanes serving as halogenating agents. A series of remotely and site‐specifically bromoalkyl‐ and chloroalkyl‐substituted quinazolin‐4(3H)‐ones were obtained with moderate yields and functional‐group tolerance. A radical pathway involving nitrogen‐centered‐radical‐induced C─C bond cleavage and carbon–halogen bond formation is proposed for this transformation.
item.content:encoded:
A cascade reaction involving spiro C—C σ–bond cleavage and C–halogen bond formation is reported for the efficient construction of bromo-/chloroalkyl-substituted quinazolin-4(3H)-ones from spiro-dihydroquinazolinones and perhalogenated methanes under organophotoredox catalysis.


Visible-light-catalyzed aromatization-driven deconstructive bromination and chlorination of spiro-dihydroquinazolinones are described, with perhalogenated methanes serving as halogenating agents. A series of remotely and site-specifically bromoalkyl- and chloroalkyl-substituted quinazolin-4(3H)-ones were obtained with moderate yields and functional-group tolerance. A radical pathway involving nitrogen-centered-radical-induced C─C bond cleavage and carbon–halogen bond formation is proposed for this transformation.

item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Catalyzed Ring‐Opening Halogenation of Spiro‐Dihydroquinazolinones Using Perhalogenated Methanes as Halogen Sources

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Homopropargyl Alcohols via Zinc Iodide Mediated Three‐Component Reaction of Propargyl Carbamates With Organoboranes and Aldehydes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This study presents a novel and practical method for synthesizing homopropargyl alcohols via a zinc iodide‐mediated three‐component reaction of terminal alkyne‐bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes under mild reaction conditions. A wide range of α,γ‐substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2‐metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom.
Homopropargyl alcohols are versatile building blocks in a wide range of synthetic transformations; consequently, the development of efficient and practical methods for their preparation remains an important objective in organic synthesis. This study presents a novel and practical zinc iodide mediated three‐component reaction of terminal alkyne‐bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes for the synthesis of homopropargyl alcohols. A variety of α,γ‐substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2‐metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom, which subsequently react with aldehydes to afford the corresponding homopropargyl alcohols. In contrast to conventional methods for the generation of alkynylborates, the present system requires only a catalytic amount of lithium diisopropylamide as the base.
item.content:encoded:

A wide range of α,γ-substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2-metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom.


Homopropargyl alcohols are versatile building blocks in a wide range of synthetic transformations; consequently, the development of efficient and practical methods for their preparation remains an important objective in organic synthesis. This study presents a novel and practical zinc iodide mediated three-component reaction of terminal alkyne-bearing propargyl carbamates, trialkylboranes, and aldehydes for the synthesis of homopropargyl alcohols. A variety of α,γ-substituted allenylboranes are formed in situ through a 1,2-metallate shift of alkynylborates to the sp carbon atom, which subsequently react with aldehydes to afford the corresponding homopropargyl alcohols. In contrast to conventional methods for the generation of alkynylborates, the present system requires only a catalytic amount of lithium diisopropylamide as the base.

item.dc:title: Synthesis of Homopropargyl Alcohols via Zinc Iodide Mediated Three‐Component Reaction of Propargyl Carbamates With Organoboranes and Aldehydes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Regioselective Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Hydroamination of Ynamides With Phenyl Isocyanates
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)‐1‐(1‐(2‐oxooxazolidin‐3‐yl)‐2‐phenylvinyl)‐1,3‐diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)‐catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed.
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)‐1‐(1‐(2‐oxooxazolidin‐3‐yl)‐2‐phenylvinyl)−1,3‐diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)‐catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed. This protocol features excellent regioselectivity with a wide range of substrate scopes of phenyl isocyanates and ynamides. Using this method, highly substituted oxooxazolidinyl phenylvinyl 1,3‐diphenylurea compounds were synthesized in moderate to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)-1-(1-(2-oxooxazolidin-3-yl)-2-phenylvinyl)-1,3-diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)-catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed.


A novel and efficient method for the synthesis of (Z)-1-(1-(2-oxooxazolidin-3-yl)-2-phenylvinyl)−1,3-diphenylurea derivatives via Ru (II)-catalyzed hydroamination of ynamides with phenyl isocyanates has been developed. This protocol features excellent regioselectivity with a wide range of substrate scopes of phenyl isocyanates and ynamides. Using this method, highly substituted oxooxazolidinyl phenylvinyl 1,3-diphenylurea compounds were synthesized in moderate to excellent yields.
item.dc:creator: Shubham Jaiswal, Amrendra Kumar, Narender Tadigoppula
item.dc:title: Highly Regioselective Ru(II)‐Catalyzed Hydroamination of Ynamides With Phenyl Isocyanates

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Silver‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Radical Aminofluorination of Arylalkenes with Selectfluor and Amino N‐Heterocycles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A three‐component aminofluorination fuses arylalkenes with Selectfluor and free amino N‐heterocycles, delivering diverse β‐fluoro heterocyclic amines in up to 98% yield.
A silver‐catalyzed three‐component radical aminofluorination of arylalkenes has been developed, utilizing free N‐heterocyclic amines and Selectfluor as key reagents. Under mild conditions, a diverse array of β‐fluoroamine derivatives were obtained in moderate to excellent yields (up to 98%). The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope, tolerating various functional groups such as halogens, alkyls, esters, and cyano groups on both the amino heterocycle and alkene components. Mechanistic studies support a radical pathway involving silver‐mediated oxidation of the amino heterocycle to generate an amino radical, followed by addition to the alkene and subsequent fluorine atom transfer. This method provides a straightforward route to valuable monofluoroalkylated amino N‐heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A three-component aminofluorination fuses arylalkenes with Selectfluor and free amino N-heterocycles, delivering diverse β-fluoro heterocyclic amines in up to 98% yield.


A silver-catalyzed three-component radical aminofluorination of arylalkenes has been developed, utilizing free N-heterocyclic amines and Selectfluor as key reagents. Under mild conditions, a diverse array of β-fluoroamine derivatives were obtained in moderate to excellent yields (up to 98%). The protocol exhibits broad substrate scope, tolerating various functional groups such as halogens, alkyls, esters, and cyano groups on both the amino heterocycle and alkene components. Mechanistic studies support a radical pathway involving silver-mediated oxidation of the amino heterocycle to generate an amino radical, followed by addition to the alkene and subsequent fluorine atom transfer. This method provides a straightforward route to valuable monofluoroalkylated amino N-heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Qiangqiang Liu, Xiao‐Lin Wang, Xiao‐Ming Zhao, Wenyan Dan, Sheng‐Cai Zheng
item.dc:title: Silver‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Radical Aminofluorination of Arylalkenes with Selectfluor and Amino N‐Heterocycles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Push–Pull Ynamines and Push–Pull Ynamides: Synthesis, Structure, Reactivity, and Application
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides, which carry an EWG and an amino or amido group as EDG, are highly interesting classes of EDG‐EWG alkynes that have been intensively studied due to their exceptional reactivity. In this review, the synthesis, structure, reactivity, and application of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynecarbamates, push–pull ynesulfonamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynesulfonamides, and push–pull enynamines are comprehensively discussed.
Push–pull ynamines, whose triple bond carries a disubstituted amino group as electron‐donating group (EDG) and an electron‐withdrawing group (EWG), are characterized by a π‐delocalization (push–pull effect) and a polarized triple bond. Push–pull ynamines exhibit the reactivity of both ynamines and EWG alkynes together with reactivities not exhibited by either class of alkyne. Their structure, in particular the push–pull effect, and high reactivity toward electrophiles, nucleophiles, and cycloaddition have been intensively studied. Push–pull oligoynamines, which carry two to four triple bonds and push–pull enynamines, which contain a double and a triple bond, are further push–pull ynamines whose synthesis, structure, and reactivity have attracted considerable interest. A second class of unique EDG‐EWG substituted alkynes are push–pull ynamides, whose triple bond holds an amido group as EDG and an EWG. Push–pull ynamides are more thermally stable but significantly less reactive than push–pull ynamines. The push–pull effect and the polarization of the triple bond are probably less pronounced in push–pull ynamides than in push–pull ynamines. Push–pull ynesulfonamides and push–pull ynecarbamates have been primarily studied in transition metal‐catalyzed reactions and cycloaddition. Keteniminium ions are key intermediates in the reaction of push–pull ynamines and push–pull ynamides with electrophiles. This review summarizes the chemistry of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynamides, and push–pull enynamines, focusing on their synthesis, structure, reactivity, and synthetic application.
item.content:encoded:

Push–pull ynamines exhibit the reactivity of both ynamines and EWG alkynes together with reactivities not exhibited by either class of alkyne. Push–pull oligoynamines, which carry two to four triple bonds and push–pull enynamines, which contain a double and a triple bond, are further push–pull ynamines whose synthesis, structure, and reactivity have attracted considerable interest. Push–pull ynesulfonamides and push–pull ynecarbamates have been primarily studied in transition metal-catalyzed reactions and cycloaddition. This review summarizes the chemistry of push–pull ynamines, push–pull ynamides, push–pull oligoynamines, push–pull oligoynamides, and push–pull enynamines, focusing on their synthesis, structure, reactivity, and synthetic application.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Hydrogen Peroxide‐Initiated Polarity‐Inversion SNAr of Halophenols with Carboxylic Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This report describes a hydrogen peroxide‐initiated SNAr reaction of halophenols with carboxylic acids using aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. The method converts halophenols into para‐acyloxy arenes under straightforward conditions, accommodates diverse carboxylic acids, and scales to gram quantities. Electrophile scope depends on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH, offering a practical carboxylate coupling strategy.
Polarity‐inversion SNAr offers a useful solution to the electronic limitations of classical two‐electron nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Here, we report a hydrogen peroxide‐initiated SNAr of halophenols with carboxylic acids using commercially available 30% aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. Under operationally straightforward conditions, this method converts readily available halophenols into para‐acyloxy arenes, accommodates a broad range of carboxylic acids, and is readily scalable to gram scale. The electrophile scope, however, shows a clear dependence on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH group. Overall, the present method provides a practical and complementary H2O2‐initiated option for thermally driven carboxylate coupling of halophenols.
item.content:encoded:
This report describes a hydrogen peroxide-initiated S N Ar reaction of halophenols with carboxylic acids using aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. The method converts halophenols into para-acyloxy arenes under straightforward conditions, accommodates diverse carboxylic acids, and scales to gram quantities. Electrophile scope depends on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH, offering a practical carboxylate coupling strategy.


Polarity-inversion S N Ar offers a useful solution to the electronic limitations of classical two-electron nucleophilic aromatic substitution. Here, we report a hydrogen peroxide-initiated S N Ar of halophenols with carboxylic acids using commercially available 30% aqueous H2O2 as a simple initiator. Under operationally straightforward conditions, this method converts readily available halophenols into para-acyloxy arenes, accommodates a broad range of carboxylic acids, and is readily scalable to gram scale. The electrophile scope, however, shows a clear dependence on ortho substitution adjacent to the phenolic OH group. Overall, the present method provides a practical and complementary H2O2-initiated option for thermally driven carboxylate coupling of halophenols.
item.dc:creator: Hui Zhao, Haofeng Shi, Lingzhi Xu, Jialiang Wu, Shuoshuo Zhang, Yunfei Du
item.dc:title: Hydrogen Peroxide‐Initiated Polarity‐Inversion SNAr of Halophenols with Carboxylic Acids

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Tri‐ and Tetrasubstituted Allyl Amines: Synthetic Strategies and Developments
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Polysubstituted allyl amines are among the important scaffolds in many natural products and biorelevant molecules. Moreover, these scaffolds are known for better pharmacological properties, metabolic stability, enhanced selectivity, and membrane permeability compared to simple allyl amines. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks confer distinctive steric and electronic characteristics, but they also pose significant synthetic challenges, such as steric congestion and difficulties in achieving precise control over regio‐ and E/Z‐selectivity. Hence, strategies for the synthesis of highly substituted counterparts remain limited. This review summarizes recent progress in the synthesis of tri‐ and tetrasubstituted allylamines, with particular emphasis on advances in transition‐metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement processes, and cross‐coupling strategies.
Highly substituted allyl amines, particularly tri‐ and tetrasubstituted variants, represent an important yet relatively underexplored class of nitrogen‐containing compounds with broad relevance in pharmaceuticals, natural product synthesis, and functional materials. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks impart unique steric and electronic features but also create substantial synthetic challenges, including steric congestion and difficulties in controlling regio‐ and E/Z‐selectivity. While mono‐ and disubstituted allyl amines are readily accessible through classical allylation and hydrofunctionalization methods, efficient and general strategies for highly substituted analogs remain limited. This review highlights recent advances in the synthesis of tri‐ and tetrasubstituted allyl amines, focusing on key developments in transition‐metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement, and cross‐coupling strategies.
item.content:encoded:

Moreover, these scaffolds are known for better pharmacological properties, metabolic stability, enhanced selectivity, and membrane permeability compared to simple allyl amines. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks confer distinctive steric and electronic characteristics, but they also pose significant synthetic challenges, such as steric congestion and difficulties in achieving precise control over regio- and E/Z-selectivity. Hence, strategies for the synthesis of highly substituted counterparts remain limited. This review summarizes recent progress in the synthesis of tri- and tetrasubstituted allylamines, with particular emphasis on advances in transition-metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement processes, and cross-coupling strategies.


Highly substituted allyl amines, particularly tri- and tetrasubstituted variants, represent an important yet relatively underexplored class of nitrogen-containing compounds with broad relevance in pharmaceuticals, natural product synthesis, and functional materials. Their densely substituted olefinic frameworks impart unique steric and electronic features but also create substantial synthetic challenges, including steric congestion and difficulties in controlling regio- and E/Z-selectivity. While mono- and disubstituted allyl amines are readily accessible through classical allylation and hydrofunctionalization methods, efficient and general strategies for highly substituted analogs remain limited. This review highlights recent advances in the synthesis of tri- and tetrasubstituted allyl amines, focusing on key developments in transition-metal catalysis, CH activation, hydrofunctionalization, rearrangement, and cross-coupling strategies.
item.dc:creator: Aradhana Sahoo, Pragati Biswal, Akhila K. Sahoo
item.dc:title: Tri‐ and Tetrasubstituted Allyl Amines: Synthetic Strategies and Developments

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Phase‐Transfer‐Catalyzed Interrupted Barton–Zard Reaction Between Electron‐Deficient Indoles and Benzophenone‐Derived Isocyanides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cascade reaction under phase‐transfer conditions between 3‐nitro indoles and isocyanomethylene dibenzenes affords the construction of pyrrolo[3,4‐b]indole cores. Control experiments provide insight into moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.
A cascade reaction enabling the construction of pyrrolo[3,4‐b]indole core from electron‐deficient and benzophenone‐derived isocyanide precursors under phase‐transfer conditions is described. This transformation proceeds in the presence of a quaternary ammonium catalyst and cesium carbonate, affording functionalized polycyclic cores. The reaction follows the interrupted Barton–Zard reaction pathway and tolerates a broad range of 3‐nitroindole and isocyanide substrates. Performed control experiments provide insight into the generally moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.
item.content:encoded:
A cascade reaction under phase-transfer conditions between 3-nitro indoles and isocyanomethylene dibenzenes affords the construction of pyrrolo[3,4-b]indole cores. Control experiments provide insight into moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.


A cascade reaction enabling the construction of pyrrolo[3,4-b]indole core from electron-deficient and benzophenone-derived isocyanide precursors under phase-transfer conditions is described. This transformation proceeds in the presence of a quaternary ammonium catalyst and cesium carbonate, affording functionalized polycyclic cores. The reaction follows the interrupted Barton–Zard reaction pathway and tolerates a broad range of 3-nitroindole and isocyanide substrates. Performed control experiments provide insight into the generally moderate product yields and clarify why the reaction is not amenable to asymmetric induction.
item.dc:creator: Ana Mikleušević, Ines Bašić, Mateja Matišić, Josip Rešetar, Matija Gredičak
item.dc:title: Phase‐Transfer‐Catalyzed Interrupted Barton–Zard Reaction Between Electron‐Deficient Indoles and Benzophenone‐Derived Isocyanides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Preparation Methods for Unsymmetrical Disulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review primarily focuses on introducing innovative methods for synthesizing unsymmetrical disulfides, utilizing six types of sulfur sources. These methods demonstrate extensive substrate applicability and good functional group tolerance under mild conditions. These innovative approaches exhibit significant advantages in terms of substrate diversity, functional group compatibility, and reaction efficiency under mild conditions, marking substantial progress compared to conventional methods. By strategically optimizing reaction parameters, the effectiveness of these synthetic protocols can be further enhanced to obtain the desired unsymmetrical disulfides.
Disulfides play pivotal roles in diverse scientific domains, ranging from protein folding and cellular redox homeostasis to advanced materials development and pharmaceutical innovation. While various synthetic methodologies have been established, the efficient and selective construction of unsymmetrical disulfides continues to present significant synthetic challenges. This review highlights innovative approaches for synthesizing unsymmetrical disulfides utilizing different sulfur sources, including thiols (or thiophenols), disulfurating reagents, and symmetrical disulfides as sulfur sources, demonstrating broad substrate applicability and functional group tolerance under mild conditions. These innovative methodologies demonstrate significant advantages in substrate diversity, functional group compatibility, and reaction efficiency under mild conditions, marking substantial progress over conventional methods. The synthetic protocols can be further enhanced through strategic optimization of reaction parameters, such as catalyst selection, substrate modification, and condition adjustment, to achieve the desired unsymmetrical disulfides. Beyond summarizing current achievements, this review outlines promising directions for future research, emphasizing the development of more sustainable and selective synthetic pathways. These advancements are anticipated to significantly contribute to the progress of pharmaceutical sciences, materials engineering, and related interdisciplinary fields, ultimately driving innovation in both fundamental research and practical applications.
item.content:encoded:

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Coupling Reactions of Iodobenzenes, an Isocyanide, and a Disilathiane: Access to Cyclic Thioimidates and Secondary Thioamides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The one‐pot synthesis of 3‐benzylidenbenzo[c]thiophen‐1‐amines was achieved through the nickel‐catalyzed three‐component coupling reaction of 1‐ethynyl‐2‐iodobenzenes, tert‐butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane. This procedure is applicable to the preparation of secondary thioamides using aryl iodides.
We demonstrate that a Ni(II)/t‐BuOK catalytic system effectively catalyzes the three‐component coupling (TCC) reactions of 2‐ethynyliodobenzenes, t‐butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane to produce various 3‐benzylidene‐N‐(t‐butyl)benzo[c]thiophen‐1‐imine derivatives with a cyclic thioimidate skeleton. The disilathiane was successfully utilized as the sulfur source and can be embedded in a ring structure. Moreover, iodobenzene can undergo the same intermolecular TCC reactions, effectively affording secondary thioamide derivatives.
item.content:encoded:
The one-pot synthesis of 3-benzylidenbenzo[c]thiophen-1-amines was achieved through the nickel-catalyzed three-component coupling reaction of 1-ethynyl-2-iodobenzenes, tert-butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane. This procedure is applicable to the preparation of secondary thioamides using aryl iodides.


We demonstrate that a Ni(II)/t-BuOK catalytic system effectively catalyzes the three-component coupling (TCC) reactions of 2-ethynyliodobenzenes, t-butyl isocyanide, and hexamethyldisilathiane to produce various 3-benzylidene-N-(t-butyl)benzo[c]thiophen-1-imine derivatives with a cyclic thioimidate skeleton. The disilathiane was successfully utilized as the sulfur source and can be embedded in a ring structure. Moreover, iodobenzene can undergo the same intermolecular TCC reactions, effectively affording secondary thioamide derivatives.
item.dc:creator: Norio Sakai, Shun Nishitai, Reo Ikeda, Yohei Ogiwara, Kento Ishida
item.dc:title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Coupling Reactions of Iodobenzenes, an Isocyanide, and a Disilathiane: Access to Cyclic Thioimidates and Secondary Thioamides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Formation of C–F Bonds by Fluorination Using Visible Light
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Visible‐light‐driven fluorination reactions have been widely developed in recent years. This review highlights recent advances in visible‐light‐mediated C–F bond formation, providing efficient strategies for the synthesis of a wide range of fluorinated compounds.
Fluorine is a widely used structural element in chemistry and has been utilized in diverse fields, including organic chemistry and pharmaceuticals. Fluorine's diverse applications have led to the development of numerous fluorination reaction methods. Visible‐light‐assisted reactions have attracted significant interest and development in organic chemistry due to their advantages in reaction conditions and efficiency. In particular, visible‐light‐driven fluorination has been recognized as a valuable synthetic strategy, resulting in the production of numerous fluorinated compounds. This review presents a summary of recent developments in visible‐light‐driven fluorination reactions developed since 2021.
item.content:encoded:

This review presents a summary of recent developments in visible-light-driven fluorination reactions developed since 2021.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Base‐Catalyzed Sulfur‐Promoted Decarboxylative Insertion of Isothiocyanates into Isatin Framework: Multicomponent Access to Quinazoline‐2,4(1H,3H)‐Dithiones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A base‐catalyzed and elemental sulfur‐promoted decarboxylative insertion of a CN motif of isothiocyanates into an isatin ring to construct quinazoline‐2,4(1H,3H)‐dithione has been described. This approach starts from easily available isatins, isothiocyanates, and sulfur, affording a series of structurally valuable heterocycles in satisfactory yields under mild conditions.
A base‐catalyzed and elemental sulfur‐promoted decarboxylative insertion of a CN motif of isothiocyanates into an isatin ring to construct quinazoline‐2,4(1H,3H)‐dithione has been described. This approach starts from easily available isatins, isothiocyanates, and sulfur, affording a series of structurally valuable heterocycles in satisfactory yields under mild conditions. Mechanism studies indicate that a base is essential to mediate the ring‐opening and thio‐Baeyer–Villiger rearrangement of isatin ring to pave the way for subsequent isothiocyanate insertion.
item.content:encoded:
A base-catalyzed and elemental sulfur-promoted decarboxylative insertion of a CN motif of isothiocyanates into an isatin ring to construct quinazoline-2,4(1H,3H)-dithione has been described. This approach starts from easily available isatins, isothiocyanates, and sulfur, affording a series of structurally valuable heterocycles in satisfactory yields under mild conditions.


A base-catalyzed and elemental sulfur-promoted decarboxylative insertion of a CN motif of isothiocyanates into an isatin ring to construct quinazoline-2,4(1H,3H)-dithione has been described. This approach starts from easily available isatins, isothiocyanates, and sulfur, affording a series of structurally valuable heterocycles in satisfactory yields under mild conditions. Mechanism studies indicate that a base is essential to mediate the ring-opening and thio-Baeyer–Villiger rearrangement of isatin ring to pave the way for subsequent isothiocyanate insertion.

item.dc:title: Base‐Catalyzed Sulfur‐Promoted Decarboxylative Insertion of Isothiocyanates into Isatin Framework: Multicomponent Access to Quinazoline‐2,4(1H,3H)‐Dithiones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sustainable Microwave‐Enhanced Synthesis of 2‐Substituted Benzofurans From o‐Alkynylanisoles in Acidic Deep Eutectic Solvents
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A microwave‐enhanced sustainable and versatile approach to substituted benzofurans from 2‐alkynylanisoles enabled by an active Brønsted acidic deep eutectic solvent.
The research on alternative media for organic transformations remains a highly demanding topic. In this paper, we describe a sustainable, microwave‐enhanced approach to 2‐substitued benzofurans starting from substituted o‐alkynylanisoles. The method involves the use of an acidic deep eutectic solvent that acts simultaneously as reaction medium and catalyst. The optimized reaction conditions are quite mild (100°C) and the process is complete within 3 h. The reaction setup is simple and does not require an inert atmosphere. Under these conditions, seventeen benzofurans were synthesized in yields of up to 90%. A plausible reaction mechanism has been proposed and supported by headspace GC analyses. To evaluate the sustainability of the process Sheldon's simple environmental factor was also calculated.
item.content:encoded:
A microwave-enhanced sustainable and versatile approach to substituted benzofurans from 2-alkynylanisoles enabled by an active Brønsted acidic deep eutectic solvent.


The research on alternative media for organic transformations remains a highly demanding topic. In this paper, we describe a sustainable, microwave-enhanced approach to 2-substitued benzofurans starting from substituted o-alkynylanisoles. The method involves the use of an acidic deep eutectic solvent that acts simultaneously as reaction medium and catalyst. The optimized reaction conditions are quite mild (100°C) and the process is complete within 3 h. The reaction setup is simple and does not require an inert atmosphere. Under these conditions, seventeen benzofurans were synthesized in yields of up to 90%. A plausible reaction mechanism has been proposed and supported by headspace GC analyses. To evaluate the sustainability of the process Sheldon's simple environmental factor was also calculated.
item.dc:creator: Alessandra Gritti, Angelica Sita, Mehri Goudarzi, Stefano Fedeli, Elison Lepore, Donatella Nava, Matteo Tiecco, Alessandro Caselli, Valentina Pirovano, Giorgio Abbiati
item.dc:title: Sustainable Microwave‐Enhanced Synthesis of 2‐Substituted Benzofurans From o‐Alkynylanisoles in Acidic Deep Eutectic Solvents

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Catalyzed Annulation Toward Fluoroalkylated Quinoxalines Using o‐Phenylenediamines and α‐Fluoroacetyl Diazoester
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed cyclization provides efficient access to fluorinated quinoxalines from o‐phenylenediamines and diazodicarbonyl compounds. Proceeding under mild conditions with broad substrate scope, this method delivers diverse derivatives in moderate to good yields, offering a streamlined approach to scaffolds valuable for medicinal and materials science applications.
A novel copper‐catalyzed cyclization provides efficient access to fluorinated quinoxalines from o‐phenylenediamines and diazodicarbonyl compounds. Proceeding under mild conditions with broad substrate scope, this protocol delivers diverse derivatives in moderate to good yields, offering a streamlined approach to scaffolds valuable for medicinal and materials science applications.

Proceeding under mild conditions with broad substrate scope, this method delivers diverse derivatives in moderate to good yields, offering a streamlined approach to scaffolds valuable for medicinal and materials science applications.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Indoloquinolinone Derivatives via Organoiodine‐Catalyzed Highly Chemoselective C−H Amidation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A novel synthetic approach for the synthesis of indoloquinolinones via organoiodine‐catalyzed intramolecular oxidative C−H amidation. The key factor in the success of this method is the introduction of a nitro group at the C5 position of the aromatic ring. This straightforward strategy offers several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, good tolerance of functional groups, and high chemoselectivity. In this work, this modification of substrates effectively reduces the electronic density of the indole, suppressing competitive side reactions and tuning the reactivity to ensure compatibility with complex oxidative conditions.
New catalytic approaches for the synthesis of indoloquinolinone derivatives have gained significant interest, as indoles are pivotal structural elements in biologically active molecules and natural products. Despite the availability of well‐established transition‐metal catalytic methods for constructing such important frameworks, the organocatalytic synthesis of indole‐based substrates is still underdeveloped and remains a formidable challenge. In this context, we have developed a highly chemoselective C−H amidation reaction catalyzed by organoiodine without dearomatization of indole‐fused reactants in a complex oxidative system. Notably, the introduction of a nitro group at the C5‐position of indole substantially enhances the reactivity of the substrates, thereby effectively accelerating the oxidative reaction under catalytic conditions. This highly selective transformation of diphenylindoles into indoloquinolinones offers a promising strategy for synthesizing diverse and bioactive indole alkaloids.
item.content:encoded:
A novel synthetic approach for the synthesis of indoloquinolinones via organoiodine-catalyzed intramolecular oxidative C−H amidation. The key factor in the success of this method is the introduction of a nitro group at the C5 position of the aromatic ring. This straightforward strategy offers several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, good tolerance of functional groups, and high chemoselectivity. In this work, this modification of substrates effectively reduces the electronic density of the indole, suppressing competitive side reactions and tuning the reactivity to ensure compatibility with complex oxidative conditions.


New catalytic approaches for the synthesis of indoloquinolinone derivatives have gained significant interest, as indoles are pivotal structural elements in biologically active molecules and natural products. Despite the availability of well-established transition-metal catalytic methods for constructing such important frameworks, the organocatalytic synthesis of indole-based substrates is still underdeveloped and remains a formidable challenge. In this context, we have developed a highly chemoselective C−H amidation reaction catalyzed by organoiodine without dearomatization of indole-fused reactants in a complex oxidative system. Notably, the introduction of a nitro group at the C5-position of indole substantially enhances the reactivity of the substrates, thereby effectively accelerating the oxidative reaction under catalytic conditions. This highly selective transformation of diphenylindoles into indoloquinolinones offers a promising strategy for synthesizing diverse and bioactive indole alkaloids.
item.dc:creator: Yu-Wei Zhang, Jiang-Ning Yu, Yue Wang, Zhao-Xia Mo, Yang Wang
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Indoloquinolinone Derivatives via Organoiodine‐Catalyzed Highly Chemoselective C−H Amidation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Total Synthesis of Koshidacin A: Payne Rearrangement–Driven Enantioselective Construction of the Side Chain Triol Unit
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A stereoselective route for the synthesis of a fungal cyclic tetrapeptide, koshidacin A, is reported. The unusual hydrophilic amino acid residue present in koshidacin A was synthesized from (S)‐homo‐Garner aldehyde using Sharpless asymmetric epoxidation and Payne rearrangement as the key steps.
Koshidacin A is a cyclic tetrapeptide isolated from the Okinawan fungus, Pochonia boninensis FKR‐0564, along with its oxidized form, Koshidacin B. These peptides fall into a series of cyclic tetrapeptides made of three hydrophobic amino acids and a fourth amino acid with a hydrophilic side chain. We report a total synthesis of Koshidacin A, which relies on the asymmetric synthesis of the hydrophilic amino acid from (S)‐homo‐Garner aldehyde and using a combination of Sharpless asymmetric epoxidation and Payne rearrangement as the key steps.
item.content:encoded:
A stereoselective route for the synthesis of a fungal cyclic tetrapeptide, koshidacin A, is reported. The unusual hydrophilic amino acid residue present in koshidacin A was synthesized from (S)-homo-Garner aldehyde using Sharpless asymmetric epoxidation and Payne rearrangement as the key steps.


Koshidacin A is a cyclic tetrapeptide isolated from the Okinawan fungus, Pochonia boninensis FKR-0564, along with its oxidized form, Koshidacin B. These peptides fall into a series of cyclic tetrapeptides made of three hydrophobic amino acids and a fourth amino acid with a hydrophilic side chain. We report a total synthesis of Koshidacin A, which relies on the asymmetric synthesis of the hydrophilic amino acid from (S)-homo-Garner aldehyde and using a combination of Sharpless asymmetric epoxidation and Payne rearrangement as the key steps.
item.dc:creator: Preeti Kapri, Devki Nandan, Ramesh Ramapanicker
item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of Koshidacin A: Payne Rearrangement–Driven Enantioselective Construction of the Side Chain Triol Unit

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: On the Planarity of Heterocyclic Bay‐Substituted Perylenediimides: Insight into the Fluorescent Photoinduced Electron Transfer Sensing Framework
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Near‐infrared fluorescent pH indicators based on perylenediimide were constructed in a modular receptor–spacer–fluorophore–spacer–receptor format with heterocyclic amino moieties at the bay positions. Counterintuitively, the dibrominated intermediate with two heavy atoms is more emissive than the heterocyclic compounds. Closer inspection of the compounds by density functional theory calculations reveals a twisted geometry between the two naphthyl subunits.
Near‐infrared fluorescent pH indicators based on perylenediimide were designed and synthesized from 1,7‐dibromo‐3,4,9,10‐perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride. The molecules were imidized with N,N‐dimethylethylenediamine and bay‐substituted with four seven‐membered heterocyclic rings of azetidine, pyrrolidine, morpholine, and azepane. Purposely constructed in a modular receptor–spacer–fluorophore–spacer–receptor format, the fluorescent pH indicators possess photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and internal charge transfer mechanisms with turn‐on emission switching in the presence of acid. Counterintuitively, the dibrominated intermediate with two heavy atoms is more brightly emissive than the heterocyclic bay‐substituted compounds. Closer inspection of the heterocyclic compounds by density functional theory calculations reveals a non‐planar bent or twisted geometry between the two naphthyl subunits. These findings highlight a notable exception to a cherished PET sensing platform, while equally providing future opportunities for the modulation of the emission by conformational tuning.
item.content:encoded:
Near-infrared fluorescent pH indicators based on perylenediimide were constructed in a modular receptor–spacer–fluorophore–spacer–receptor format with heterocyclic amino moieties at the bay positions. Counterintuitively, the dibrominated intermediate with two heavy atoms is more emissive than the heterocyclic compounds. Closer inspection of the compounds by density functional theory calculations reveals a twisted geometry between the two naphthyl subunits.


Near-infrared fluorescent pH indicators based on perylenediimide were designed and synthesized from 1,7-dibromo-3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride. The molecules were imidized with N,N-dimethylethylenediamine and bay-substituted with four seven-membered heterocyclic rings of azetidine, pyrrolidine, morpholine, and azepane. Purposely constructed in a modular receptor–spacer–fluorophore–spacer–receptor format, the fluorescent pH indicators possess photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and internal charge transfer mechanisms with turn-on emission switching in the presence of acid. Counterintuitively, the dibrominated intermediate with two heavy atoms is more brightly emissive than the heterocyclic bay-substituted compounds. Closer inspection of the heterocyclic compounds by density functional theory calculations reveals a non-planar bent or twisted geometry between the two naphthyl subunits. These findings highlight a notable exception to a cherished PET sensing platform, while equally providing future opportunities for the modulation of the emission by conformational tuning.
item.dc:creator: Luke Camenzuli, Davide Benedetto Tiz, Nicola’ Agius, Konrad Szaciłowski, David C. Magri
item.dc:title: On the Planarity of Heterocyclic Bay‐Substituted Perylenediimides: Insight into the Fluorescent Photoinduced Electron Transfer Sensing Framework

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Configurational Reassignment of (+)‐Polycitorol A and (−)‐Polycitorol B by Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopic Analysis, Theoretical Calculation, and Total Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The configurations of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, were reassigned through nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic analysis, theoretical calculation, and total synthesis. The stereochemistry of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitorol B corresponded to that of (−)‐fasicularin.
The proposed structures of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, have been questioned by Kim et al. through total synthesis. In this study, the structure of polycitorol A, a perhydropyrrolo[2,1‐j]quinoline derivative, was scrutinized by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic analysis and theoretical calculation. DP4+ analysis of the density functional theory‐calculated NMR chemical shifts showed that the proposed structure most likely required configurational reassignment at the C2 and C5 positions. The reassigned structure of (+)‐polycitorol A was finally established through total synthesis. Inspired by known interconversion between cylindricines A and B through an aziridinium ion, a ring‐expansion reaction of (+)‐polycitorol A under Cossy conditions led to (−)‐polycitorol B. The stereochemistry of (+)‐polycitorol A and (−)‐polycitrol B corresponded to that of (−)‐fasicularin.
item.content:encoded:
The configurations of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, were reassigned through nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic analysis, theoretical calculation, and total synthesis. The stereochemistry of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitorol B corresponded to that of (−)-fasicularin.


The proposed structures of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitorol B, marine tricyclic alkaloids, have been questioned by Kim et al. through total synthesis. In this study, the structure of polycitorol A, a perhydropyrrolo[2,1-j]quinoline derivative, was scrutinized by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic analysis and theoretical calculation. DP4+ analysis of the density functional theory-calculated NMR chemical shifts showed that the proposed structure most likely required configurational reassignment at the C2 and C5 positions. The reassigned structure of (+)-polycitorol A was finally established through total synthesis. Inspired by known interconversion between cylindricines A and B through an aziridinium ion, a ring-expansion reaction of (+)-polycitorol A under Cossy conditions led to (−)-polycitorol B. The stereochemistry of (+)-polycitorol A and (−)-polycitrol B corresponded to that of (−)-fasicularin.
item.dc:creator: Kyoko Tatsumoto, Keisuke Murata, Keita Ishikawa, Haruhiko Fuwa
item.dc:title: Configurational Reassignment of (+)‐Polycitorol A and (−)‐Polycitorol B by Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopic Analysis, Theoretical Calculation, and Total Synthesis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Base‐Mediated Synthesis of Sulfur‐Substituted Imides using N‐(acyloxy)‐Tetrachlorophthalimides/Anhydrides and Alkyl Isothiouronium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An efficient, scalable, and versatile base‐triggered synthesis of sulfur‐substituted imides with the utility of alkyl isothiouronium salts and masked carboxylic acids under mild reaction conditions.
Imides have a vast presence in pharmaceuticals and natural products, and their synthesis generally relies on the utility of the disagreeable strong oxidants. Herein, we report an oxidant‐free protocol that combines N‐(acyloxy)‐tetrachlorophthalimides, derived from abundant carboxylic acids, and acid anhydrides with isothiouronium salts. Crucial to this protocol is the provision of access to sulfur‐substituted imides, which were previously limited to sulfur‐β‐allyl‐substituted imides and the use of undesirable free thiols. To this end, this developed protocol provides a scalable and functional group‐tolerant reactivity with product yields of up to 96% under mild reaction conditions.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient, scalable, and versatile base-triggered synthesis of sulfur-substituted imides with the utility of alkyl isothiouronium salts and masked carboxylic acids under mild reaction conditions.


Imides have a vast presence in pharmaceuticals and natural products, and their synthesis generally relies on the utility of the disagreeable strong oxidants. Herein, we report an oxidant-free protocol that combines N-(acyloxy)-tetrachlorophthalimides, derived from abundant carboxylic acids, and acid anhydrides with isothiouronium salts. Crucial to this protocol is the provision of access to sulfur-substituted imides, which were previously limited to sulfur-β-allyl-substituted imides and the use of undesirable free thiols. To this end, this developed protocol provides a scalable and functional group-tolerant reactivity with product yields of up to 96% under mild reaction conditions.
item.dc:creator: Malibongwe P. Shandu, Andile R. Ngwenya, Madelien Wooding, Jairus L. Lamola, Paseka T. Moshapo
item.dc:title: Base‐Mediated Synthesis of Sulfur‐Substituted Imides using N‐(acyloxy)‐Tetrachlorophthalimides/Anhydrides and Alkyl Isothiouronium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Carbene‐Catalyzed Enantioselective Synthesis of 1‐Oxyacylated‐2,4‐Disubstituted Cyclopentenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A carbene‐catalyzed enantioselective synthesis of multi‐functionalized oxyacylated cyclopentenes through [3 + 2] cycloaddition of α‐functionalized vinyl ketones and enals has been reported
Herein, we report the first carbene‐catalyzed enantioselective synthesis of multi‐functionalized oxyacylated cyclopentenes through [3 + 2] cycloaddition of α‐functionalized vinyl ketones and enals. The trisubstituted cyclopentenes were obtained with excellent enantioselectivity that could be readily converted to functionalized cyclopentenols.
item.content:encoded:
A carbene-catalyzed enantioselective synthesis of multi-functionalized oxyacylated cyclopentenes through [3 + 2] cycloaddition of α-functionalized vinyl ketones and enals has been reported


Herein, we report the first carbene-catalyzed enantioselective synthesis of multi-functionalized oxyacylated cyclopentenes through [3 + 2] cycloaddition of α-functionalized vinyl ketones and enals. The trisubstituted cyclopentenes were obtained with excellent enantioselectivity that could be readily converted to functionalized cyclopentenols.
item.dc:creator: Hemlata Mourya, Kajal Verma, Bal Krishna Mishra, Bhoopendra Tiwari
item.dc:title: Carbene‐Catalyzed Enantioselective Synthesis of 1‐Oxyacylated‐2,4‐Disubstituted Cyclopentenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Promoted, (Photo)catalyst‐Free Synthesis of 3‐Sulfonyl‐Azaspiro[4.5]trienones via Arylazo Sulfones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A set of 3‐sulfonyl‐azaspiro[4.5]trienones have been obtained via a metal‐ and photocatalyst‐free visible‐light‐driven dearomative strategy. The protocol exploits arylazo sulfones, which act simultaneously as the light absorber and sulfonyl‐radical source, enabling ipso‐spirocyclization of differently substituted N‐aryl propiolamides.
Here, 3‐sulfonyl‐azaspiro[4.5]trienones are now accessible via a versatile visible‐light‐driven dearomative strategy under metal‐ and (photo)catalyst‐free conditions, starting from differently substituted N‐aryl propiolamides and arylazo sulfones. The protocol employs ethyl acetate as a benign solvent and tert‐butyl hydroperoxide as the oxidant. The reaction proceeds through direct photoexcitation of the arylazo sulfone, which acts simultaneously as a light absorber and a sulfonyl‐radical source, enabling ipso‐spirocyclization of aromatic amides. This transformation combines a broad substrate scope with mild conditions and operational simplicity, providing a valuable synthetic entry to sulfur‐containing spiroheterocycles of pharmacological interest.
item.content:encoded:
A set of 3-sulfonyl-azaspiro[4.5]trienones have been obtained via a metal- and photocatalyst-free visible-light-driven dearomative strategy. The protocol exploits arylazo sulfones, which act simultaneously as the light absorber and sulfonyl-radical source, enabling ipso-spirocyclization of differently substituted N-aryl propiolamides.


Here, 3-sulfonyl-azaspiro[4.5]trienones are now accessible via a versatile visible-light-driven dearomative strategy under metal- and (photo)catalyst-free conditions, starting from differently substituted N-aryl propiolamides and arylazo sulfones. The protocol employs ethyl acetate as a benign solvent and tert-butyl hydroperoxide as the oxidant. The reaction proceeds through direct photoexcitation of the arylazo sulfone, which acts simultaneously as a light absorber and a sulfonyl-radical source, enabling ipso-spirocyclization of aromatic amides. This transformation combines a broad substrate scope with mild conditions and operational simplicity, providing a valuable synthetic entry to sulfur-containing spiroheterocycles of pharmacological interest.
item.dc:creator: Luca Nicchio, Giulia Pompilio, Maurizio Fagnoni, Luc Neuville, Géraldine Masson, Stefano Protti
item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Promoted, (Photo)catalyst‐Free Synthesis of 3‐Sulfonyl‐Azaspiro[4.5]trienones via Arylazo Sulfones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Near‐Infrared‐Absorbing α‐Functionalized TCBD‐BODIPYs: Synthesis, Photophysical, and Electrochemical Properties
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A set of α‐functionalized meso‐anisyl boron‐dipyrromethene (BODIPYs) was synthesized via Sonogashira cross‐coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition‐retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl‐bridged α‐BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono‐1,1,4,4‐tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)‐incorporated α‐BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) were investigated. The TCBD‐incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band spanning 620–1000 nm.
Boron‐dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes absorb in the visible region; however, expanding their optical absorption into the near‐infrared (NIR) window is beneficial for optoelectronic applications. Herein, we report a set of α‐functionalized meso‐anisyl BODIPYs, BDS1–BDS4, synthesized via Sonogashira cross‐coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition‐retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl‐bridged α‐BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono‐1,1,4,4‐tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)‐incorporated α‐BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) are investigated. The TCBD‐incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band between 620 and 1000 nm, with an absorption maximum above 700 nm. The TCBD‐BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) display about 40 nm red shift in absorption maxima compared to the ethynyl‐bridged BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3). The electrochemical studies reveal that the TCBD unit in BDS2 and BDS4 significantly lowers the LUMO energy levels, leading to smaller energy gaps for BDS2 and BDS4 compared to their corresponding ethynyl‐bridged BODIPYs, BDS1 and BDS3. The computational studies were performed to optimize the geometry and estimate the electrostatic potential of the α‐BODIPYs. This study highlights the effect of an additional acceptor TCBD on the photophysical and electrochemical properties of α‐functionalized BODIPYs, providing valuable insights for designing new NIR‐absorbing molecules for optoelectronic applications.
item.content:encoded:
A set of α-functionalized meso-anisyl boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPYs) was synthesized via Sonogashira cross-coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition-retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl-bridged α-BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono-1,1,4,4-tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)-incorporated α-BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) were investigated. The TCBD-incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band spanning 620–1000 nm.


Boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes absorb in the visible region; however, expanding their optical absorption into the near-infrared (NIR) window is beneficial for optoelectronic applications. Herein, we report a set of α-functionalized meso-anisyl BODIPYs, BDS1–BDS4, synthesized via Sonogashira cross-coupling and [2 + 2] cycloaddition-retroelectrocyclization reaction. The photophysical, electrochemical, and computational studies of the ethynyl-bridged α-BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3) and mono-1,1,4,4-tetracyanobutadiene (TCBD)-incorporated α-BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) are investigated. The TCBD-incorporated BODIPYs exhibit a broad absorption band between 620 and 1000 nm, with an absorption maximum above 700 nm. The TCBD-BODIPYs (BDS2 and BDS4) display about 40 nm red shift in absorption maxima compared to the ethynyl-bridged BODIPYs (BDS1 and BDS3). The electrochemical studies reveal that the TCBD unit in BDS2 and BDS4 significantly lowers the LUMO energy levels, leading to smaller energy gaps for BDS2 and BDS4 compared to their corresponding ethynyl-bridged BODIPYs, BDS1 and BDS3. The computational studies were performed to optimize the geometry and estimate the electrostatic potential of the α-BODIPYs. This study highlights the effect of an additional acceptor TCBD on the photophysical and electrochemical properties of α-functionalized BODIPYs, providing valuable insights for designing new NIR-absorbing molecules for optoelectronic applications.

item.dc:title: Near‐Infrared‐Absorbing α‐Functionalized TCBD‐BODIPYs: Synthesis, Photophysical, and Electrochemical Properties

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Eight‐Membered Carbocycle‐Fused 1,2,3‐Triazole Scaffolds via Palladium‐Catalyzed Tandem Reactions and Michael Addition
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A palladium‐catalyzed tandem reaction of 2‐iodophenyl‐enones and propargylic ethers is developed to provide the rapid construction of a fused eight‐membered carbocyclic ring‐1,2,3‐triazole scaffold. Furthermore, we demonstrate that subsequent base addition plays a crucial role in converting the Sonogashira cross‐coupling intermediate into the target product.
Both eight‐membered carbocyclic rings and 1,2,3‐triazole rings represent vital structural motifs. In this article, we present a Pd‐catalyzed tandem reaction that facilitates the synthesis of fused eight membered carbocyclic ring‐fused 1,2,3‐triazole derivatives from 2‐iodophenyl‐enones and propargylic ethers bearing azidophenyl group. Furthermore, we show that TBD plays a pivotal role as a mediator in converting the Sonogashira cross‐coupling intermediates into the desired products.
item.content:encoded:
A palladium-catalyzed tandem reaction of 2-iodophenyl-enones and propargylic ethers is developed to provide the rapid construction of a fused eight-membered carbocyclic ring-1,2,3-triazole scaffold. Furthermore, we demonstrate that subsequent base addition plays a crucial role in converting the Sonogashira cross-coupling intermediate into the target product.


Both eight-membered carbocyclic rings and 1,2,3-triazole rings represent vital structural motifs. In this article, we present a Pd-catalyzed tandem reaction that facilitates the synthesis of fused eight membered carbocyclic ring-fused 1,2,3-triazole derivatives from 2-iodophenyl-enones and propargylic ethers bearing azidophenyl group. Furthermore, we show that TBD plays a pivotal role as a mediator in converting the Sonogashira cross-coupling intermediates into the desired products.
item.dc:creator: Xinxin Chen, Yu Xin, Yuxuan Fang, Bin Liu, Ruwei Shen, Shugao Zhu
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Eight‐Membered Carbocycle‐Fused 1,2,3‐Triazole Scaffolds via Palladium‐Catalyzed Tandem Reactions and Michael Addition

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pd(II)/Ce(III)‐Heterobimetallic Complex‐Catalyzed Decarbonylative Alkyne Annulation Reaction: Synthesis of Spiro‐Benzofuranindenones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd(II) and Ce(III)‐catalyzed decarbonylative alkyne annulation reaction was developed for the efficient synthesis of spiro‐benzofuranindenones.
The decarbonylation of 3‐hydroxy‐2‐phenyl‐chromenones and subsequent annulation with disubstituted alkyne in the presence of Pd(OAc)2 (5.0 mol%) and Ce(OAc)3 (10.0 mol%) provided an efficient route for the synthesis of spiro‐benzofuranindenones. This reaction could be used for the synthesis of wide range of spiro‐benzofuranindenones with good yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd(II) and Ce(III)-catalyzed decarbonylative alkyne annulation reaction was developed for the efficient synthesis of spiro-benzofuranindenones.


The decarbonylation of 3-hydroxy-2-phenyl-chromenones and subsequent annulation with disubstituted alkyne in the presence of Pd(OAc)2 (5.0 mol%) and Ce(OAc)3 (10.0 mol%) provided an efficient route for the synthesis of spiro-benzofuranindenones. This reaction could be used for the synthesis of wide range of spiro-benzofuranindenones with good yields.
item.dc:creator: Bondana Bora, Kongkona Gogoi, Sanjib Gogoi
item.dc:title: Pd(II)/Ce(III)‐Heterobimetallic Complex‐Catalyzed Decarbonylative Alkyne Annulation Reaction: Synthesis of Spiro‐Benzofuranindenones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Total Synthesis of All (8E)‐Stereoisomers of Panaxjapyne B
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Stereoselective total synthesis of all four possible (8E)‐stereoisomers of panaxjapyne B were accomplished and the comparative analysis revealed three of the synthesized products correspond to the natural products isolated from various sources. The synthesis involves copper(I) mediated regio‐selective ring opening of (R)‐ and (S)‐ TBS glycidyl ethers, base‐induced elimination of β‐alkoxy iodide and Cadiot‐Chodkiewicz cross‐coupling reaction as the key steps.
The first stereoselective total synthesis of all possible (8E)‐stereoisomers of panaxjapyne B was accomplished in a convergent fashion. The synthesis involves copper(I)‐mediated regioselective Grignard opening of (R)‐ and (S)‐OTBS glycidyl ethers, base‐induced elimination of β‐alkoxy iodides followed by Cadiot–Chodkiewicz cross‐coupling reaction as the key steps. D‐(−)‐Diethyl tartrate and both (R)‐ and (S)‐OTBS glycidyl ethers were used as chiral pool starting materials for the construction of the crucial chiral ene‐yne and chiral bromoalkyne fragments. Comparative analysis revealed that, out of all four possible (8E)‐stereoisomers of panaxjapyne B, three isomers correspond to natural products.
item.content:encoded:
Stereoselective total synthesis of all four possible (8E)-stereoisomers of panaxjapyne B were accomplished and the comparative analysis revealed three of the synthesized products correspond to the natural products isolated from various sources. The synthesis involves copper(I) mediated regio-selective ring opening of (R)- and (S)- TBS glycidyl ethers, base-induced elimination of β-alkoxy iodide and Cadiot-Chodkiewicz cross-coupling reaction as the key steps.


The first stereoselective total synthesis of all possible (8E)-stereoisomers of panaxjapyne B was accomplished in a convergent fashion. The synthesis involves copper(I)-mediated regioselective Grignard opening of (R)- and (S)-OTBS glycidyl ethers, base-induced elimination of β-alkoxy iodides followed by Cadiot–Chodkiewicz cross-coupling reaction as the key steps. D-(−)-Diethyl tartrate and both (R)- and (S)-OTBS glycidyl ethers were used as chiral pool starting materials for the construction of the crucial chiral ene-yne and chiral bromoalkyne fragments. Comparative analysis revealed that, out of all four possible (8E)-stereoisomers of panaxjapyne B, three isomers correspond to natural products.
item.dc:creator: Pamarthi Gangadhar, Sayini Ramakrishna, Pabbaraja Srihari
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Total Synthesis of All (8E)‐Stereoisomers of Panaxjapyne B
